text.NRSV: A cheerful heart is a good medicine, but a downcast spirit dries up the bones.
text.Rotherham: A joyful heart, worketh an excellent cure,—but, a stricken spirit, drieth up the bone.
text.KJV: A merry heart doeth good like a medicine: but a broken spirit drieth the bones.
text.NABRE: A joyful heart is the health of the body, but a depressed spirit dries up the bones.
text.DRB: Ajoyful mind maketh a flourishing age, a sorrowful spirit dryeth up the bones.

text.NRSV: The wicked accept a concealed bribe to pervert the ways of justice.
text.Rotherham: A bribe out of his bosom, doth a lawless man take, to pervert the ways of justice.
text.KJV: A wicked man taketh a gift out of the bosom to pervert the ways of judgment.
text.NABRE: A guilty person takes out a bribe from the pocket, thus perverting the course of justice.
text.DRB: The impious receiveth gifts out of the bosom, that he may pervert the paths of judgment.

text.NRSV: The person of understanding sets his face toward wisdom, but the eyes of a fool are on the ends of the earth.
text.Rotherham: Before the face of the discerning, is wisdom, but, the eyes of a dullard, are in the ends of the earth.
text.KJV: Wisdom is before him that hath understanding; but the eyes of a fool are in the ends of the earth.
text.NABRE: On the countenance of a discerning person is wisdom, but the eyes of a fool are on the ends of the earth.
text.DRB: In the face of the prudent wisdom shineth: the eyes of fools are in the ends of the earth.

text.NRSV: A foolish child is a grief to a father and bitterness to her who bore him.
text.Rotherham: A vexation to his father, is the son that is a dullard, and a bitterness, to her that bare him.
text.KJV: A foolish son is a grief to his father, and bitterness to her that bare him.
text.NABRE: A foolish son is vexation to his father, and bitter sorrow to her who bore him.
text.DRB: Afoolish son is the anger of the father: and the sorrow of the mother that bare him.

text.NRSV: To impose a fine on the innocent is not right, or to flog the noble for their integrity.
text.Rotherham: Surely, to chastise the righteous, is not good, to smite the noble-minded for equity.
text.KJV: Also to punish the just is not good, nor to strike princes for equity.
text.NABRE: It is wrong to fine an innocent person, but beyond reason to scourge nobles.
text.DRB: It is not good to do hurt to the just: nor to strike the Prince, which judgeth right.

text.NRSV: One who spares words is knowledgeable; one who is cool in spirit has understanding.
text.Rotherham: Sparing of his words, is one who valueth knowledge, and, of a thoughtful spirit, is a man of intelligence.
text.KJV: He that hath knowledge spareth his words: and a man of understanding is of an excellent spirit.
text.NABRE: Those who spare their words are truly knowledgeable, and those who are discreet are intelligent.
text.DRB: He that moderateth his words, is learned and prudent: and the learned man is of a precious spirit.

text.NRSV: Even fools who keep silent are considered wise; when they close their lips, they are deemed intelligent.
text.Rotherham: Even a fool, holding his peace, is accounted, wise,—He that closeth his lips, [is thought] to have understanding.
text.KJV: Even a fool, when he holdeth his peace, is counted wise: and he that shutteth his lips is esteemed a man of understanding.
text.NABRE: Even fools, keeping silent, are considered wise; if they keep their lips closed, intelligent.
text.DRB: The fool also if he hold his peace, shall be reputed wise: and if he close his lips, a man of understanding.

text.NRSV: The one who lives alone is self-indulgent, showing contempt for all who have sound judgment.
text.Rotherham: A man seeketh, for satisfaction, going his own way, through all safe counsel, he breaketh.
text.KJV: Through desire a man, having separated himself, seeketh and intermeddleth with all wisdom.
text.NABRE: Chapter 18 One who is alienated seeks a pretext, with all persistence picks a quarrel.
text.DRB: He seeketh occasions that will depart from a friend: he shall ever be subject to reproach.

text.NRSV: A fool takes no pleasure in understanding, but only in baring his mind.
text.Rotherham: A dullard, delighteth not, in understanding, in nothing save the exposing of his own heart.
text.KJV: A fool hath no delight in understanding, but that his heart may discover itself.
text.NABRE: Fools take no delight in understanding, but only in displaying what they think.
text.DRB: Afool receiveth not the words of prudence: unless thou say those things which are in his heart.

text.NRSV: When wickedness comes, contempt comes also; and with dishonor comes disgrace.
text.Rotherham: When the lawless man cometh in, then cometh also contempt, and, with shame, reproach.
text.KJV: When the wicked cometh, then cometh also contempt, and with ignominy reproach.
text.NABRE: With wickedness comes contempt, and with disgrace, scorn.
text.DRB: The impious when he shall come into the depth of sins, contemneth: but ignominy and reproach follow him.

text.NRSV: The words of the mouth are deep waters; the fountain of wisdom is a gushing stream.
text.Rotherham: Deep waters, are the words of a man’s mouth,—a bubbling brook, the well-spring of wisdom.
text.KJV: The words of a man’s mouth are as deep waters, and the wellspring of wisdom as a flowing brook.
text.NABRE: The words of one’s mouth are deep waters, the spring of wisdom, a running brook.
text.DRB: Deep water words from the mouth of a man: and a stream overflowing the fountain of wisdom.

text.NRSV: It is not right to be partial to the wicked, or to subvert the innocent in judgment.
text.Rotherham: To prefer a lawless man, is not good, thrusting away the righteous, in judgment.
text.KJV: It is not good to accept the person of the wicked, to overthrow the righteous in judgment.
text.NABRE: It is not good to favor the guilty, nor to reject the claim of the just.
text.DRB: To accept the person of the impious in judgment is not good, that thou decline from the truth of judgment.

text.NRSV: A fool’s lips bring strife, and a fool’s mouth invites a flogging.
text.Rotherham: the lips of a dullard, enter into contention, and his mouth, for blows, crieth out.
text.KJV: A fool’s lips enter into contention, and his mouth calleth for strokes.
text.NABRE: The lips of fools walk into a fight, and their mouths are asking for a beating.
text.DRB: The lips of a fool mingle him with strife: and his mouth provoketh brawls.

text.NRSV: The mouths of fools are their undoing, and their lips are a snare to themselves.
text.Rotherham: The mouth of a dullard, is his ruin, and, his lips, are a snare to his soul.
text.KJV: A fool’s mouth is his destruction, and his lips are the snare of his soul.
text.NABRE: The mouths of fools are their ruin; their lips are a deadly snare.
text.DRB: The mouth of a fool is his destruction: and his lips are the ruin of his soul.

text.NRSV: The words of a whisperer are like delicious morsels; they go down into the inner parts of the body.
text.Rotherham: the words of a tattler, are dainties, they, therefore go down into the chambers of the inner man.
text.KJV: The words of a talebearer are as wounds, and they go down into the innermost parts of the belly.
text.NABRE: The words of a talebearer are like dainty morsels: they sink into one’s inmost being.
text.DRB: The words of the double tongued, as it were simple: and the same come even to the inner part of the belly. Fear casteth down the slothful: and the souls of the effeminate shall be hungry.

text.NRSV: One who is slack in work is close kin to a spoiler.
text.Rotherham: Surely he that is slothful in his work, brother, is he to a master at laying waste.
text.KJV: He also that is slothful in his work is brother to him that is a great waster.
text.NABRE: Those slack in their work are kin to the destroyer.
text.DRB: He that is soft and dissolute in his work, is the brother of him that destroyeth his own works.

text.NRSV: The name of the Lord is a strong tower; the righteous run into it and are safe.
text.Rotherham: A tower of strength, is the Name of Yahweh, thereinto, runneth the righteous, and is safe.
text.KJV: The name of the Lord is a strong tower: the righteous runneth into it, and is safe.
text.NABRE: The name of the Lord is a strong tower; the just run to it and are safe.
text.DRB: Amost strong tower, the name of our Lord: the just runneth to it, and shall be exalted.

text.NRSV: The wealth of the rich is their strong city; in their imagination it is like a high wall.
text.Rotherham: The substance of a rich man, is his strong city, and like a high wall, in his imagination.
text.KJV: The rich man’s wealth is his strong city, and as an high wall in his own conceit.
text.NABRE: The wealth of the rich is their strong city; they fancy it a high wall.
text.DRB: The substance of the rich man a city of his strength: and as a strong wall compassing him about.

text.NRSV: Before destruction one’s heart is haughty, but humility goes before honor.
text.Rotherham: Before grievous injury, a man’s heart is haughty, and, before honour, is humility.
text.KJV: Before destruction the heart of man is haughty, and before honour is humility.
text.NABRE: Before disaster the heart is haughty, but before honor is humility.
text.DRB: Before he be broken, the heart of a man is exalted: and before he be glorified, it is humbled.

text.NRSV: If one gives answer before hearing, it is folly and shame.
text.Rotherham: He that answereth before he heareth, a folly, it is to him, and, a reproach.
text.KJV: He that answereth a matter before he heareth it, it is folly and shame unto him.
text.NABRE: Whoever answers before listening, theirs is folly and shame.
text.DRB: He that answereth before he hear, sheweth himself to be a fool, and worthy of confusion.

text.NRSV: The human spirit can endure a physical sickness, but a broken spirit who can bear?
text.Rotherham: The spirit of a man, sustaineth his sickness, but, a dejected spirit, who can bear it?
text.KJV: The spirit of a man will sustain his infirmity; but a wounded spirit who can bear?
text.NABRE: One’s spirit supports one when ill, but a broken spirit who can bear?
text.DRB: The spirit of a man upholdeth his imbecility: but a spirit that is easy to be angry who can sustain?

text.NRSV: An intelligent mind acquires knowledge, and the ear of the wise seeks knowledge.
text.Rotherham: the heart of the intelligent, acquireth knowledge, yea, the ear of the wise, seeketh knowledge.
text.KJV: The heart of the prudent getteth knowledge; and the ear of the wise seeketh knowledge.
text.NABRE: The heart of the intelligent acquires knowledge, and the ear of the wise seeks knowledge.
text.DRB: Awise heart shall possess knowledge: and the ear of the wise seeketh doctrine.

text.NRSV: A gift opens doors; it gives access to the great.
text.Rotherham: The gift of a man, maketh room for him, and, before great men, setteth him down.
text.KJV: A man’s gift maketh room for him, and bringeth him before great men.
text.NABRE: Gifts clear the way for people, winning access to the great.
text.DRB: The gift of a man enlargeth his way, & maketh him room before Princes.

text.NRSV: The one who first states a case seems right, until the other comes and cross-examines.
text.Rotherham: Righteous is he that is first in his own cause, then cometh his neighbour, and thoroughly searcheth him.
text.KJV: He that is first in his own cause seemeth just; but his neighbour cometh and searcheth him.
text.NABRE: Those who plead the case first seem to be in the right; then the opponent comes and cross-examines them.
text.DRB: The just is first accuser of himself: his friend cometh, and shall search him.

text.NRSV: Casting the lot puts an end to disputes and decides between powerful contenders.
text.Rotherham: The lot causeth, contentions, to cease, and, the mighty, it parteth.
text.KJV: The lot causeth contentions to cease, and parteth between the mighty.
text.NABRE: The lot puts an end to disputes, and decides a controversy between the mighty.
text.DRB: Lot suppresseth contradictions, and between the mighty also it determineth.

text.NRSV: An ally offended is stronger than a city; such quarreling is like the bars of a castle.
text.Rotherham: A brother estranged, [is worse] than a strong city,—and, contentions, are as the bar of a citadel.
text.KJV: A brother offended is harder to be won than a strong city: and their contentions are like the bars of a castle.
text.NABRE: A brother offended is more unyielding than a stronghold; such strife is more daunting than castle gates.
text.DRB: Brother that is holpen of brother, is a strong city: and judgments are as the bars of cities.

text.NRSV: From the fruit of the mouth one’s stomach is satisfied; the yield of the lips brings contentment.
text.Rotherham: Of the fruit of a man’s mouth, shall his inmost mind be satisfied, with the product of his lips, shall he be satisfied.
text.KJV: A man’s belly shall be satisfied with the fruit of his mouth; and with the increase of his lips shall he be filled.
text.NABRE: With the fruit of one’s mouth one’s belly is filled, with the produce of one’s lips one is sated.
text.DRB: Of the fruit of man's mouth his belly shall be filled: and the offsprings of his lips shall fill him.

text.NRSV: Death and life are in the power of the tongue, and those who love it will eat its fruits.
text.Rotherham: Death and life, are in the power of the tongue, and, its friends, shall eat its fruits.
text.KJV: Death and life are in the power of the tongue: and they that love it shall eat the fruit thereof.
text.NABRE: Death and life are in the power of the tongue; those who choose one shall eat its fruit.
text.DRB: Death and life in the hand of the tongue: they that love it, shall eat the fruits thereof.

text.NRSV: He who finds a wife finds a good thing, and obtains favor from the Lord.
text.Rotherham: Who hath found a wife, hath found a blessing, and hath obtained favour from Yahweh.
text.KJV: Whoso findeth a wife findeth a good thing, and obtaineth favour of the Lord.
text.NABRE: To find a wife is to find happiness, a favor granted by the Lord.
text.DRB: He that hath found a good wife, hath found a good thing, and hath received a pleasure of our Lord.

text.NRSV: The poor use entreaties, but the rich answer roughly.
text.Rotherham: Supplications, doth the poor man utter, but, the rich, answereth fiercely.
text.KJV: The poor useth intreaties; but the rich answereth roughly.
text.NABRE: The poor implore, but the rich answer harshly.
text.DRB: The poor speaketh with supplications, and the rich will speak roughly.

text.NRSV: Some friends play at friendship but a true friend sticks closer than one’s nearest kin.
text.Rotherham: A man having [many] friends, shall come to ruin, but there is a loving one, who sticketh closer than a brother.
text.KJV: A man that hath friends must shew himself friendly: and there is a friend that sticketh closer than a brother.
text.NABRE: There are friends who bring ruin, but there are true friends more loyal than a brother.
text.DRB: Aman amiable to society, shall be more friendly than a brother.

text.NRSV: Better the poor walking in integrity than one perverse of speech who is a fool.
text.Rotherham: Better is a poor man, walking in his integrity, than one of perverse lips, and, he, a dullard.
text.KJV: Better is the poor that walketh in his integrity, than he that is perverse in his lips, and is a fool.
text.NABRE: Chapter 19 Better to be poor and walk in integrity than rich and crooked in one’s ways.
text.DRB: Better is a poor man, that walketh in his simplicity, than a rich writhing his lips, and unwise.

text.NRSV: Desire without knowledge is not good, and one who moves too hurriedly misses the way.
text.Rotherham: Surely, for the soul to be, without knowledge, is not good, and, he that hasteth with his feet, strayeth.
text.KJV: Also, that the soul be without knowledge, it is not good; and he that hasteth with his feet sinneth.
text.NABRE: Desire without knowledge is not good; and whoever acts hastily, blunders.
text.DRB: Where is no knowledge of the soul, is not good: and he that is hasty with his feet shall stumble.

text.NRSV: One’s own folly leads to ruin, yet the heart rages against the Lord.
text.Rotherham: The folly of a man, subverteth his way, and, against Yahweh, his heart is angry.
text.KJV: The foolishness of man perverteth his way: and his heart fretteth against the Lord.
text.NABRE: Their own folly leads people astray; in their hearts they rage against the Lord.
text.DRB: The folly of a man supplanteth his steps: and he boileth in his mind against God.

text.NRSV: Wealth brings many new friends, but the poor are left friendless.
text.Rotherham: Wealth, addeth many friends, but, the poor man, from his own friend, is parted.
text.KJV: Wealth maketh many friends; but the poor is separated from his neighbour.
text.NABRE: Wealth adds many friends, but the poor are left friendless.
text.DRB: Riches add many friends: but from the poor they also which he had are separated.

text.NRSV: A false witness will not go unpunished, and one who utters lies will not escape.
text.Rotherham: A false witness, shall not be held innocent, and, he that uttereth lies, shall not escape.
text.KJV: A false witness shall not be unpunished, and he that speaketh lies shall not escape.
text.NABRE: The false witness will not go unpunished, and whoever utters lies will not escape.
text.DRB: Afalse witness shall not be unpunished: & he that speaketh lies, shall not escape.

text.NRSV: Many seek the favor of a generous person, and everyone is a friend to a giver of gifts.
text.Rotherham: Many, will entreat the favour of a noble, and, every one, is a friend to a man abounding in gifts.
text.KJV: Many will intreat the favour of the prince: and every man is a friend to him that giveth gifts.
text.NABRE: Many curry favor with a noble; everybody is a friend of a gift giver.
text.DRB: Many worship the person of the mighty, and are friends of him that giveth gifts.

text.NRSV: If all the kindred of the poor hate them, how much more do their friends avoid them! They may help with words, but such are not to be found.
text.Rotherham: All the brethren of a poor man, hate him, how much more have, his friends, gone far from him, he may hunt up promises—there are none.
text.KJV: All the brethren of the poor do hate him: how much more do his friends go far from him? he pursueth them with words, yet they are wanting to him.
text.NABRE: All the kin of the poor despise them; how much more do their friends shun them!
text.DRB: The brethren of the poor man hate him: moreover also his friends have departed far from him.

text.NRSV: To get wisdom is to love oneself; to keep understanding is to prosper.
text.Rotherham: He that acquireth sense, loveth his own soul, he that guardeth understanding, shall find blessing.
text.KJV: He that getteth wisdom loveth his own soul: he that keepeth understanding shall find good.
text.NABRE: Those who gain sense truly love themselves; those who preserve understanding will find success.
text.DRB: He that pursueth words only, shall have nothing: but he that is possessor of the mind, loveth his soul, and the keeper of prudence shall find good things.

text.NRSV: A false witness will not go unpunished, and one who utters lies will perish.
text.Rotherham: A false witness, shall not be held innocent, and, he that uttereth lies, shall perish.
text.KJV: A false witness shall not be unpunished, and he that speaketh lies shall perish.
text.NABRE: The false witness will not go unpunished, and whoever utters lies will perish.
text.DRB: Afalse witness shall not be unpunished: and he that speaketh lies shall perish.

text.NRSV: It is not fitting for a fool to live in luxury, much less for a slave to rule over princes.
text.Rotherham: Unseemly for a dullard, is delicate living, how much more for, a servant, to bear rule over princes.
text.KJV: Delight is not seemly for a fool; much less for a servant to have rule over princes.
text.NABRE: Luxury is not befitting a fool; much less should a slave rule over princes.
text.DRB: Delicacies become not a fool: nor a servant to rule over Princes.

text.NRSV: Those with good sense are slow to anger, and it is their glory to overlook an offense.
text.Rotherham: The discretion of a man, deferreth his anger, and, his adorning, is to pass over transgression.
text.KJV: The discretion of a man deferreth his anger; and it is his glory to pass over a transgression.
text.NABRE: It is good sense to be slow to anger, and an honor to overlook an offense.
text.DRB: The doctrine of man is known by patience: and his glory is to overpass unjust things.

text.NRSV: A king’s wrath is like the growling of a lion, but his favor is like dew on the grass.
text.Rotherham: The growl as of a young lion, is the rage of a king, but, like dew upon the grass, is his good pleasure.
text.KJV: The king’s wrath is as the roaring of a lion; but his favour is as dew upon the grass.
text.NABRE: The king’s wrath is like the roar of a lion, but his favor, like dew on the grass.
text.DRB: As the roaring of a lion, so also the anger of a king: and as dew upon grass, so also his cheerfulness.

text.NRSV: A foolish child is ruin to a father, and a wife’s quarreling is a continual dripping of rain.
text.Rotherham: Engulfing ruin to his father, is a son that is a dullard,—and, a continuous dripping, are the contentions of a wife.
text.KJV: A foolish son is the calamity of his father: and the contentions of a wife are a continual dropping.
text.NABRE: The foolish son is ruin to his father, and a quarrelsome wife is water constantly dripping.
text.DRB: The sorrow of the father, a foolish son: and roofs continually dropping through, a woman full of brawling.

text.NRSV: House and wealth are inherited from parents, but a prudent wife is from the Lord.
text.Rotherham: House and substance, are an inheritance from one’s fathers, but, from Yahweh, cometh a wife who is prudent.
text.KJV: House and riches are the inheritance of fathers: and a prudent wife is from the Lord.
text.NABRE: Home and possessions are an inheritance from parents, but a prudent wife is from the Lord.
text.DRB: House and riches are given of the parents: but of our Lord properly a prudent wife.

text.NRSV: Laziness brings on deep sleep; an idle person will suffer hunger.
text.Rotherham: Sloth, falleth into a deep sleep, and, a soul that is indolent, shall famish.
text.KJV: Slothfulness casteth into a deep sleep; and an idle soul shall suffer hunger.
text.NABRE: Laziness brings on deep sleep, and the sluggard goes hungry.
text.DRB: Slothfulness bringeth drowsiness, and a dissolute soul shall be an hungered.

text.NRSV: Those who keep the commandment will live; those who are heedless of their ways will die.
text.Rotherham: One who guardeth the commandment, guardeth his life, he that is reckless in his ways, shall die.
text.KJV: He that keepeth the commandment keepeth his own soul; but he that despiseth his ways shall die.
text.NABRE: Those who keep commands keep their lives, but those who despise these ways will die.
text.DRB: He that keepeth the commandment, keepeth his soul: but he that neglecteth his way, shall die.

text.NRSV: Whoever is kind to the poor lends to the Lord, and will be repaid in full.
text.Rotherham: A lender to Yahweh, is one who sheweth favour to the poor, and, his good deed, He will pay him back.
text.KJV: He that hath pity upon the poor lendeth unto the Lord; and that which he hath given will he pay him again.
text.NABRE: Whoever cares for the poor lends to the Lord, who will pay back the sum in full.
text.DRB: He lendeth our Lord that hath mercy on the poor: and he will repay him the like.

text.NRSV: Discipline your children while there is hope; do not set your heart on their destruction.
text.Rotherham: Correct thy son, because there is hope, yet, not so as to slay him, let thy passion be excited.
text.KJV: Chasten thy son while there is hope, and let not thy soul spare for his crying.
text.NABRE: Discipline your son, for there is hope; but do not be intent on his death.
text.DRB: Nurture thy son, despair not: but to the killing of him set not thy soul.

text.NRSV: A violent tempered person will pay the penalty; for if you effect a rescue, you will only have to do it again.
text.Rotherham: A man in a rage, taketh away the penalty,—nevertheless, if thou let him go free, the more [stripes] must thou add.
text.KJV: A man of great wrath shall suffer punishment: for if thou deliver him, yet thou must do it again.
text.NABRE: A wrathful person bears the penalty; after one rescue, you will have it to do again.
text.DRB: He that is impatient shall sustain damage: and when he shall take away violently, he shall add another thing.

text.NRSV: Listen to advice and accept instruction, that you may gain wisdom for the future.
text.Rotherham: Hear counsel, receive correction, that thou mayest be wise in thine after-life.
text.KJV: Hear counsel, and receive instruction, that thou mayest be wise in thy latter end.
text.NABRE: Listen to counsel and receive instruction, that you may eventually become wise.
text.DRB: Hear counsel, and receive discipline, that thou mayst be wise in thy later ends.

text.NRSV: The human mind may devise many plans, but it is the purpose of the Lord that will be established.
text.Rotherham: Many are the devices in a man’s heart, but, the counsel of Yahweh, that, shall stand.
text.KJV: There are many devices in a man’s heart; nevertheless the counsel of the Lord, that shall stand.
text.NABRE: Many are the plans of the human heart, but it is the decision of the Lord that endures.
text.DRB: Many cogitations in the heart of a man: but the will of our Lord shall be permanent.

text.NRSV: What is desirable in a person is loyalty, and it is better to be poor than a liar.
text.Rotherham: The charm of a man, is his lovingkindness,—and better a poor man, than one who deceiveth.
text.KJV: The desire of a man is his kindness: and a poor man is better than a liar.
text.NABRE: What is desired of a person is fidelity; rather be poor than a liar.
text.DRB: Aneedy man is merciful: and better is the poor than the lying man.

text.NRSV: The fear of the Lord is life-giving; one rests satisfied and untouched by harm.
text.Rotherham: The reverence of Yahweh, leadeth to life, satisfied then, let a man remain—let him not be visited by calamity.
text.KJV: The fear of the Lord tendeth to life: and he that hath it shall abide satisfied; he shall not be visited with evil.
text.NABRE: The fear of the Lord leads to life; one eats and sleeps free from any harm.
text.DRB: The fear of our Lord unto life: and in fullness he shall abide, without the visitation most noisome:

text.NRSV: The lazy person buries a hand in the dish, and will not even bring it back to the mouth.
text.Rotherham: A sluggard burieth his hand in the dish, even unto his own mouth, will he not bring it back.
text.KJV: A slothful man hideth his hand in his bosom, and will not so much as bring it to his mouth again.
text.NABRE: The sluggard buries a hand in the dish; not even lifting it to the mouth.
text.DRB: The slothful hideth his hand under the arm-hole, neither doth he put it to his mouth.

text.NRSV: Strike a scoffer, and the simple will learn prudence; reprove the intelligent, and they will gain knowledge.
text.Rotherham: The scoffer, thou shalt smite, and, the simple, will beware, but correct a man of intelligence—he will understand teaching.
text.KJV: Smite a scorner, and the simple will beware: and reprove one that hath understanding, and he will understand knowledge.
text.NABRE: Beat a scoffer and the naive learn a lesson; rebuke the intelligent and they gain knowledge.
text.DRB: The pestilent man being whipped, the fool shall be wiser: but if thou rebuke a wiseman he will understand discipline.

text.NRSV: Those who do violence to their father and chase away their mother are children who cause shame and bring reproach.
text.Rotherham: He that ruineth his father, and chaseth away his mother, is a son causing shame and reproach.
text.KJV: He that wasteth his father, and chaseth away his mother, is a son that causeth shame, and bringeth reproach.
text.NABRE: Whoever mistreats a father or drives away a mother, is a shameless and disgraceful child.
text.DRB: He that afflicteth his father, and flyeth from his mother, is ignominious and unhappy.

text.NRSV: Cease straying, my child, from the words of knowledge, in order that you may hear instruction.
text.Rotherham: Cease, my son, to hear instruction that would cause thee to wander from the sayings of knowledge.
text.KJV: Cease, my son, to hear the instruction that causeth to err from the words of knowledge.
text.NABRE: My son, stop attending to correction; start straying from words of knowledge.
text.DRB: Cease not, o son, to hear doctrine, neither be ignorant of the words of knowledge.

text.NRSV: A worthless witness mocks at justice, and the mouth of the wicked devours iniquity.
text.Rotherham: An abandoned witness, scorneth justice, and, the mouth of lawless men, swalloweth iniquity.
text.KJV: An ungodly witness scorneth judgment: and the mouth of the wicked devoureth iniquity.
text.NABRE: An unprincipled witness scoffs at justice, and the mouth of the wicked pours out iniquity.
text.DRB: An unjust witness scorneth judgment: and the mouth of the impious devoureth iniquity.

text.NRSV: Condemnation is ready for scoffers, and flogging for the backs of fools.
text.Rotherham: Prepared for scoffers, are punishments, and stripes, for the back of dullards.
text.KJV: Judgments are prepared for scorners, and stripes for the back of fools.
text.NABRE: Rods are prepared for scoffers, and blows for the backs of fools.
text.DRB: Judgments are prepared for scorners: & hammers striking for the bodies of fools.

text.NRSV: Wine is a mocker, strong drink a brawler, and whoever is led astray by it is not wise.
text.Rotherham: Wine is, a scoffer, and strong drink, a brawler, every one therefore who erreth therein, is unwise.
text.KJV: Wine is a mocker, strong drink is raging: and whosoever is deceived thereby is not wise.
text.NABRE: Chapter 20 Wine is arrogant, strong drink is riotous; none who are intoxicated by them are wise.
text.DRB: Wine is a luxurious thing, & drunkenness tumultuous: whosoever is delighted therewith shall not be wise.

text.NRSV: The dread of a king is like the growling of a lion; anyone who provokes him to anger forfeits life itself.
text.Rotherham: The growl as of a young lion, is the dread inspired by a king, he that provoketh him, endangereth his own life.
text.KJV: The fear of a king is as the roaring of a lion: whoso provoketh him to anger sinneth against his own soul.
text.NABRE: The terror of a king is like the roar of a lion; those who incur his anger forfeit their lives.
text.DRB: As the roaring of a lion, so also the terror of a king: he that provoketh him, sinneth also against his own soul.

text.NRSV: It is an honor to abstain from strife, but every fool is quick to quarrel.
text.Rotherham: Honour, hath the man who sitteth away from strife, but, any fool, may break through.
text.KJV: It is an honour for a man to cease from strife: but every fool will be meddling.
text.NABRE: A person gains honor by avoiding strife, while every fool starts a quarrel.
text.DRB: It is honour to a man that separateth himself from contentions: but all fools meddle with contumelies.

text.NRSV: The lazy person does not plow in season; harvest comes, and there is nothing to be found.
text.Rotherham: By reason of the autumn, a sluggard will not plough, therefore shall he beg in harvest, and there be nothing.
text.KJV: The sluggard will not plow by reason of the cold; therefore shall he beg in harvest, and have nothing.
text.NABRE: In seedtime sluggards do not plow; when they look for the harvest, it is not there.
text.DRB: Because of cold the slothful would not plow: he shall beg therefore in the summer, and it shall not be given him.

text.NRSV: The purposes in the human mind are like deep water, but the intelligent will draw them out.
text.Rotherham: Deep water, is counsel in a man’s heart, but, a man of understanding, will draw it out.
text.KJV: Counsel in the heart of man is like deep water; but a man of understanding will draw it out.
text.NABRE: The intention of the human heart is deep water, but the intelligent draw it forth.
text.DRB: As deep water, so counsel in the heart of a man: but a wise man shall draw it out.

text.NRSV: Many proclaim themselves loyal, but who can find one worthy of trust?
text.Rotherham: A kind man one may call, a great man,—but, a faithful man, who can find?
text.KJV: Most men will proclaim every one his own goodness: but a faithful man who can find?
text.NABRE: Many say, “My loyal friend,” but who can find someone worthy of trust?
text.DRB: Many men are called merciful: but a faithful man who shall find?

text.NRSV: The righteous walk in integrity—happy are the children who follow them!
text.Rotherham: As for a righteous man, walking in his integrity, how happy are his children after him!
text.KJV: The just man walketh in his integrity: his children are blessed after him.
text.NABRE: The just walk in integrity; happy are their children after them!
text.DRB: The just that walketh in his simplicity, shall leave blessed children.

text.NRSV: A king sitting on the throne of judgment winnows all evil with his eyes.
text.Rotherham: A king sitting on the throne of judgment, scattereth, with his eyes, all wrong.
text.KJV: A king that sitteth in the throne of judgment scattereth away all evil with his eyes.
text.NABRE: A king seated on the throne of judgment dispels all evil with his glance.
text.DRB: The king that sitteth in the throne of judgment, dissipateth all evil with his look.

text.NRSV: Who can say, “I have made my heart clean; I am pure from my sin”?
text.Rotherham: Who can say, I have cleansed my heart, I am pure from my sin?
text.KJV: Who can say, I have made my heart clean, I am pure from my sin?
text.NABRE: Who can say, “I have made my heart clean, I am cleansed of my sin”?
text.DRB: Who can say: My heart is clean, I am pure from sin?

text.NRSV: Diverse weights and diverse measures are both alike an abomination to the Lord.
text.Rotherham: Divers weights, and divers measures, an abomination to Yahweh, are they, both.
text.KJV: Divers weights, and divers measures, both of them are alike abomination to the Lord.
text.NABRE: Varying weights, varying measures, are both an abomination to the Lord.
text.DRB: Weight and weight, measure and measure: both are abominable before God.

text.NRSV: Even children make themselves known by their acts, by whether what they do is pure and right.
text.Rotherham: Even, by his doings, doth a youth make himself known, whether, pure and upright, be his work.
text.KJV: Even a child is known by his doings, whether his work be pure, and whether it be right.
text.NABRE: In their actions even children can playact though their deeds be blameless and right.
text.DRB: By his conversation a child is perceived, if his works be clean and right.

text.NRSV: The hearing ear and the seeing eye—the Lord has made them both.
text.Rotherham: The hearing ear, and the seeing eye, Yahweh hath made them, both.
text.KJV: The hearing ear, and the seeing eye, the Lord hath made even both of them.
text.NABRE: The ear that hears, the eye that sees— the Lord has made them both.
text.DRB: The ear hearing, and the eye seeing, our Lord made both.

text.NRSV: Do not love sleep, or else you will come to poverty; open your eyes, and you will have plenty of bread.
text.Rotherham: Do not love sleep, lest thou come to poverty, open thine eyes, be satisfied with bread.
text.KJV: Love not sleep, lest thou come to poverty; open thine eyes, and thou shalt be satisfied with bread.
text.NABRE: Do not love sleep lest you be reduced to poverty; keep your eyes open, have your fill of food.
text.DRB: Love not sleep, lest poverty oppress thee: open thine eyes and be filled with breads.

text.NRSV: “Bad, bad,” says the buyer, then goes away and boasts.
text.Rotherham: Bad! bad! saith the buyer, but, going his way, then, he boasteth.
text.KJV: It is naught, it is naught, saith the buyer: but when he is gone his way, then he boasteth.
text.NABRE: “Bad, bad!” says the buyer, then goes away only to boast.
text.DRB: It is naught, it is naught, saith every buyer: and when he is departed he will boast.

text.NRSV: There is gold, and abundance of costly stones; but the lips of knowledge are a precious jewel.
text.Rotherham: There are gold, and an abundance of corals, but, precious jewels, are the lips of knowledge.
text.KJV: There is gold, and a multitude of rubies: but the lips of knowledge are a precious jewel.
text.NABRE: One can put on gold and abundant jewels, but wise lips are the most precious ornament.
text.DRB: There is gold, and multitude of pearls: but a precious vessel the lips of knowledge.

text.NRSV: Take the garment of one who has given surety for a stranger; seize the pledge from those who are foreigners.
text.Rotherham: Take his garment who is pledge for a stranger,—then, for a woman unknown, accept him as surety.
text.KJV: Take his garment that is surety for a stranger: and take a pledge of him for a strange woman.
text.NABRE: Take the garment of the one who became surety for a stranger; if for foreigners, exact the pledge!
text.DRB: Take his garment, that was the surety of a stranger, and for strangers take a pledge from him.

text.NRSV: Bread gained by deceit is sweet, but afterward the mouth will be full of gravel.
text.Rotherham: Sweet to a man, may be the bread of falsehood, but, afterward, shall his mouth be filled with gravel.
text.KJV: Bread of deceit is sweet to a man; but afterwards his mouth shall be filled with gravel.
text.NABRE: Bread earned by deceit is sweet, but afterward the mouth is filled with gravel.
text.DRB: The bread of lying is sweet to a man: and afterward his mouth shall be filled with the gravel stone.

text.NRSV: Plans are established by taking advice; wage war by following wise guidance.
text.Rotherham: Plans—by counsel, shalt thou establish, and, with concerted measures, make thou war.
text.KJV: Every purpose is established by counsel: and with good advice make war.
text.NABRE: Plans made with advice succeed; with wise direction wage your war.
text.DRB: Cogitations are strengthened by counsels: and battles are to be handled by governments.

text.NRSV: A gossip reveals secrets; therefore do not associate with a babbler.
text.Rotherham: A revealer of secrets, is one who goeth about talebearing, therefore, with him who openeth his lips, shalt thou not have fellowship.
text.KJV: He that goeth about as a talebearer revealeth secrets: therefore meddle not with him that flattereth with his lips.
text.NABRE: A slanderer reveals secrets; so have nothing to do with a babbler!
text.DRB: Meddle not with him that revealeth mysteries, and walketh fraudulently, and dilateth his lips.

text.NRSV: If you curse father or mother, your lamp will go out in utter darkness.
text.Rotherham: Whoso revileth his father or his mother, his lamp shall be put out in deep darkness.
text.KJV: Whoso curseth his father or his mother, his lamp shall be put out in obscure darkness.
text.NABRE: Those who curse father or mother— their lamp will go out in the dead of night.
text.DRB: He that curseth his father, and mother, his lamp shall be extinguished in the midst of darkness.

text.NRSV: An inheritance quickly acquired at the beginning will not be blessed in the end.
text.Rotherham: An inheritance hastily gotten at the beginning, the latter end thereof, shall not bring blessing.
text.KJV: An inheritance may be gotten hastily at the beginning; but the end thereof shall not be blessed.
text.NABRE: Possessions greedily guarded at the outset will not be blessed in the end.
text.DRB: The inheritance whereunto haste is made in the beginning, in the later end shall lack blessing.

text.NRSV: Do not say, “I will repay evil”; wait for the Lord, and he will help you.
text.Rotherham: Do not say, I will requite wrong! Wait thou for Yahweh that he may save thee.
text.KJV: Say not thou, I will recompense evil; but wait on the Lord, and he shall save thee.
text.NABRE: Do not say, “I will repay evil!” Wait for the Lord, who will help you.
text.DRB: Say not, I will requite evil: expect our Lord, and he will deliver thee.

text.NRSV: Diverse weights are an abomination to the Lord, and false scales are not good.
text.Rotherham: An abomination to Yahweh, are divers weights, and, deceptive balances, are not good.
text.KJV: Divers weights are an abomination unto the Lord; and a false balance is not good.
text.NABRE: Varying weights are an abomination to the Lord, and false scales are not good.
text.DRB: Weight and weight are abomination with our Lord: a deceitful balance is not good.

text.NRSV: All our steps are ordered by the Lord; how then can we understand our own ways?
text.Rotherham: From Yahweh, are a man’s steps, a son of earth, then—how can he discern his way?
text.KJV: Man’s goings are of the Lord; how can a man then understand his own way?
text.NABRE: Our steps are from the Lord; how, then, can mortals understand their way?
text.DRB: The steps of man are directed of our Lord: but who of men can understand his own way?

text.NRSV: It is a snare for one to say rashly, “It is holy,” and reflect only after making a vow.
text.Rotherham: It is a snare to a man, that he should rashly cry Holy! and, after making vows, to reflect!
text.KJV: It is a snare to the man who devoureth that which is holy, and after vows to make enquiry.
text.NABRE: It is a trap to pledge rashly a sacred gift, and after a vow, then to reflect.
text.DRB: It is ruin to a man to devour saints, and afterward to retract the vows.

text.NRSV: A wise king winnows the wicked, and drives the wheel over them.
text.Rotherham: A wise king, winnoweth out the lawless, when he hath turned over them the wheel.
text.KJV: A wise king scattereth the wicked, and bringeth the wheel over them.
text.NABRE: A wise king winnows the wicked, and threshes them under the cartwheel.
text.DRB: Awise king dissipateth the impious, and bendeth over them a triumphant arch.

text.NRSV: The human spirit is the lamp of the Lord, searching every inmost part.
text.Rotherham: The lamp of Yahweh, is the spirit of a son of earth, searching all the chambers of the inner man.
text.KJV: The spirit of man is the candle of the Lord, searching all the inward parts of the belly.
text.NABRE: A lamp from the Lord is human life-breath; it searches through the inmost being.
text.DRB: The lamp of our Lord, the breath of a man, which searcheth all the secrets of the belly.

text.NRSV: Loyalty and faithfulness preserve the king, and his throne is upheld by righteousness.
text.Rotherham: Lovingkindness and faithfulness, will guard a king,—therefore should he support, with lovingkindness, his throne.
text.KJV: Mercy and truth preserve the king: and his throne is upholden by mercy.
text.NABRE: His steadfast loyalty safeguards the king, and he upholds his throne by justice.
text.DRB: Mercy & truth keep the king, and his throne is strengthened by clemency.

text.NRSV: The glory of youths is their strength, but the beauty of the aged is their gray hair.
text.Rotherham: The beauty of young men, is their strength, and, the ornament of old men, a hoary head.
text.KJV: The glory of young men is their strength: and the beauty of old men is the gray head.
text.NABRE: The glory of the young is their strength, and the dignity of the old is gray hair.
text.DRB: The joy of youngmen their strength: and the dignity of oldmen a gray head.

text.NRSV: Blows that wound cleanse away evil; beatings make clean the inmost parts.
text.Rotherham: Blows that cut in, cleanse away wickedness, and, smitings, [enter] the chambers of the inner man.
text.KJV: The blueness of a wound cleanseth away evil: so do stripes the inward parts of the belly.
text.NABRE: Evil is cleansed away by bloody lashes, and a scourging to the inmost being.
text.DRB: The blueness of the wound shall wipe away evils: and stripes in the more secret place of the belly.

text.NRSV: The king’s heart is a stream of water in the hand of the Lord; he turns it wherever he will.
text.Rotherham: [Like] channels of water, is the heart of a king, in the hand of Yahweh,—whithersoever he will, he turneth it.
text.KJV: The king’s heart is in the hand of the Lord, as the rivers of water: he turneth it whithersoever he will.
text.NABRE: Chapter 21 A king’s heart is channeled water in the hand of the Lord; God directs it where he pleases.
text.DRB: As divisions of waters, so the heart of the king is in the hand of our Lord: whither soever he will, he shall incline it.

text.NRSV: All one’s ways may be right in one’s own eyes, but the Lord weighs the heart.
text.Rotherham: Every way of a man, may be right in his own eyes, but, he that testeth hearts, is Yahweh.
text.KJV: Every way of a man is right in his own eyes: but the Lord pondereth the hearts.
text.NABRE: All your ways may be straight in your own eyes, but it is the Lord who weighs hearts.
text.DRB: Every way of a man seemeth to himself right: but our Lord weigheth the hearts.

text.NRSV: To do righteousness and justice is more acceptable to the Lord than sacrifice.
text.Rotherham: To do righteousness and justice, is more choice to Yahweh than sacrifice.
text.KJV: To do justice and judgment is more acceptable to the Lord than sacrifice.
text.NABRE: To do what is right and just is more acceptable to the Lord than sacrifice.
text.DRB: To do mercy and judgment, doth more please our Lord than victims.

text.NRSV: Haughty eyes and a proud heart—the lamp of the wicked—are sin.
text.Rotherham: Loftiness of eyes, and ambition of heart—the lamp of the lawless, are sin.
text.KJV: An high look, and a proud heart, and the plowing of the wicked, is sin.
text.NABRE: Haughty eyes and a proud heart— the lamp of the wicked will fail.
text.DRB: Exaltation of the eyes is the dilation of the heart: the lamp of the impious sin.

text.NRSV: The plans of the diligent lead surely to abundance, but everyone who is hasty comes only to want.
text.Rotherham: The plans of the diligent, tend only to abundance, but, of every one that is urgent, only to want.
text.KJV: The thoughts of the diligent tend only to plenteousness; but of every one that is hasty only to want.
text.NABRE: The plans of the diligent end in profit, but those of the hasty end in loss.
text.DRB: The cogitations of the strong are always in aboundance: but every sluggard is always in poverty.

text.NRSV: The getting of treasures by a lying tongue is a fleeting vapor and a snare of death.
text.Rotherham: The gaining of treasures with a tongue of falsehood, is a vapour driven away, [they who seek them] seek death,
text.KJV: The getting of treasures by a lying tongue is a vanity tossed to and fro of them that seek death.
text.NABRE: Trying to get rich by lying is chasing a bubble over deadly snares.
text.DRB: He that gathereth treasures with a lying tongue is vain and witless, and shall stumble at the snares of death.

text.NRSV: The violence of the wicked will sweep them away, because they refuse to do what is just.
text.Rotherham: The violence of the lawless, shall drag them away,—because they have refused to do justice.
text.KJV: The robbery of the wicked shall destroy them; because they refuse to do judgment.
text.NABRE: The violence of the wicked will sweep them away, because they refuse to do what is right.
text.DRB: The robberies of the impious shall draw them down, because they would not do judgment.

text.NRSV: The way of the guilty is crooked, but the conduct of the pure is right.
text.Rotherham: Crooked is the way of a guilty man, but, as for the pure, straight is his dealing.
text.KJV: The way of man is froward and strange: but as for the pure, his work is right.
text.NABRE: One’s path may be winding and unfamiliar, but one’s conduct is blameless and right.
text.DRB: The perverse way of a man is strange: but he that is clean, his work is right.

text.NRSV: It is better to live in a corner of the housetop than in a share of a house with a contentious woman.
text.Rotherham: Better to dwell on the corner of the roof, than a quarrelsome wife, and a house in common.
text.KJV: It is better to dwell in a corner of the housetop, than with a brawling woman in a wide house.
text.NABRE: It is better to dwell in a corner of the housetop than in a mansion with a quarrelsome woman.
text.DRB: It is better to sit in a corner of the house top, than with a brawling woman, and in a common house.

text.NRSV: The souls of the wicked desire evil; their neighbors find no mercy in their eyes.
text.Rotherham: The soul of the lawless man, craveth mischief, his own friend, findeth no favour in his eyes.
text.KJV: The soul of the wicked desireth evil: his neighbour findeth no favour in his eyes.
text.NABRE: The soul of the wicked desires evil; their neighbor finds no pity in their eyes.
text.DRB: The soul of the impious desireth evil, he will not have pity on his neighbour.

text.NRSV: When a scoffer is punished, the simple become wiser; when the wise are instructed, they increase in knowledge.
text.Rotherham: When the scoffer is punished, the simple, becometh wise, when the wise is instructed, he receiveth knowledge.
text.KJV: When the scorner is punished, the simple is made wise: and when the wise is instructed, he receiveth knowledge.
text.NABRE: When scoffers are punished the naive become wise; when the wise succeed, they gain knowledge.
text.DRB: The pestilent man being punished, the little one will be wiser: and if he follow the wiseman, he will take knowledge.

text.NRSV: The Righteous One observes the house of the wicked; he casts the wicked down to ruin.
text.Rotherham: The Righteous One observeth the house of the lawless,—He is ready to cast down lawless men into misfortune.
text.KJV: The righteous man wisely considereth the house of the wicked: but God overthroweth the wicked for their wickedness.
text.NABRE: The Righteous One appraises the house of the wicked, bringing down the wicked to ruin.
text.DRB: The just deviseth concerning the house of the impious, that he may draw the impious from evil.

text.NRSV: If you close your ear to the cry of the poor, you will cry out and not be heard.
text.Rotherham: He that shutteth his ear from the cry of the poor, even he, shall call, and not be answered.
text.KJV: Whoso stoppeth his ears at the cry of the poor, he also shall cry himself, but shall not be heard.
text.NABRE: Those who shut their ears to the cry of the poor will themselves call out and not be answered.
text.DRB: He that stoppeth his ear at the cry of the poor, himself also shall cry and shall not be heard.

text.NRSV: A gift in secret averts anger; and a concealed bribe in the bosom, strong wrath.
text.Rotherham: A gift in secret, quencheth anger, and, a present in the bosom, mighty wrath.
text.KJV: A gift in secret pacifieth anger: and a reward in the bosom strong wrath.
text.NABRE: A secret gift allays anger, and a present concealed, violent wrath.
text.DRB: Agift hid quencheth angers: and a gift in the bosom the greatest indignation.

text.NRSV: When justice is done, it is a joy to the righteous, but dismay to evildoers.
text.Rotherham: It is a joy, to a righteous man, to do justice, but, dismay, to the workers of iniquity.
text.KJV: It is joy to the just to do judgment: but destruction shall be to the workers of iniquity.
text.NABRE: When justice is done it is a joy for the just, downfall for evildoers.
text.DRB: It is a joy to the just to do judgment: and dread to them that work iniquity.

text.NRSV: Whoever wanders from the way of understanding will rest in the assembly of the dead.
text.Rotherham: The man who wandereth from the way of discretion, in the gathered host of the shades, shall settle down.
text.KJV: The man that wandereth out of the way of understanding shall remain in the congregation of the dead.
text.NABRE: Whoever strays from the way of good sense will abide in the assembly of the shades.
text.DRB: Aman, that shall err from the way of doctrine, shall abide in the assembly of giants.

text.NRSV: Whoever loves pleasure will suffer want; whoever loves wine and oil will not be rich.
text.Rotherham: A needy man, shall he be that loveth merriment, the lover of wine and oil, shall not become rich.
text.KJV: He that loveth pleasure shall be a poor man: he that loveth wine and oil shall not be rich.
text.NABRE: The lover of pleasure will suffer want; the lover of wine and perfume will never be rich.
text.DRB: He that loveth good cheer, shall be in poverty: he that loveth wine, and fat things, shall not be rich.

text.NRSV: The wicked is a ransom for the righteous, and the faithless for the upright.
text.Rotherham: A ransom for the righteous, is the lawless, and, instead of upright men, the traitor.
text.KJV: The wicked shall be a ransom for the righteous, and the transgressor for the upright.
text.NABRE: The wicked serve as ransom for the just, and the faithless for the upright.
text.DRB: The impious shall be given for the just: and the unjust for the righteous.

text.NRSV: It is better to live in a desert land than with a contentious and fretful woman.
text.Rotherham: Better to dwell in a desert land, than with a woman, quarrelsome and provoking.
text.KJV: It is better to dwell in the wilderness, than with a contentious and an angry woman.
text.NABRE: It is better to dwell in a wilderness than with a quarrelsome wife and trouble.
text.DRB: It is better to dwell in a desert land, than with a brawling and angry woman.

text.NRSV: Precious treasure and oil are in the house of the wise, but the fool devours them.
text.Rotherham: Desirable treasure and oil, are in the home of the wise, but, a man who is a dullard, will destroy it.
text.KJV: There is treasure to be desired and oil in the dwelling of the wise; but a foolish man spendeth it up.
text.NABRE: Precious treasure and oil are in the house of the wise, but the fool consumes them.
text.DRB: Treasure to be desired, and oil in the habitation of the just: and the unwise man shall dissipate it.

text.NRSV: Whoever pursues righteousness and kindness will find life and honor.
text.Rotherham: He that pursueth righteousness and lovingkindness, shall find life, righteousness and honour.
text.KJV: He that followeth after righteousness and mercy findeth life, righteousness, and honour.
text.NABRE: Whoever pursues justice and kindness will find life and honor.
text.DRB: He that followeth justice and mercy, shall find life, justice, and glory.

text.NRSV: One wise person went up against a city of the mighty and brought down the stronghold in which they trusted.
text.Rotherham: A city of heroes, doth a wise man scale, and bringeth down the strength of its confidence.
text.KJV: A wise man scaleth the city of the mighty, and casteth down the strength of the confidence thereof.
text.NABRE: The wise person storms the city of the mighty, and overthrows the stronghold in which they trust.
text.DRB: The wise hath scaled the city of the strong, and hath destroyed the confidence thereof.

text.NRSV: To watch over mouth and tongue is to keep out of trouble.
text.Rotherham: He that keepeth his mouth and his tongue, keepeth, out of distresses, his soul.
text.KJV: Whoso keepeth his mouth and his tongue keepeth his soul from troubles.
text.NABRE: Those who guard mouth and tongue guard themselves from trouble.
text.DRB: He that keepeth his mouth, and his tongue, keepeth his soul from distresses.

text.NRSV: “Proud,” “Haughty,” “Scoffer” are the names of those who act with overbearing pride.
text.Rotherham: A haughty insolent one—Scoffer is his name, is he that acteth in a transport of pride.
text.KJV: Proud and haughty scorner is his name, who dealeth in proud wrath.
text.NABRE: Proud, boastful—scoffer is the name: those who act with overbearing pride.
text.DRB: The proud and arrogant is called unlearned, which in anger worketh pride.

text.NRSV: The craving of the lazy person is fatal, for lazy hands refuse to labor.
text.Rotherham: The craving of the sluggard, killeth him, for his hands have refused to work.
text.KJV: The desire of the slothful killeth him; for his hands refuse to labour.
text.NABRE: The desire of sluggards will slay them, for their hands refuse to work.
text.DRB: Desires kill the slothful: for his hands would not work any thing:

text.NRSV: All day long the wicked covet, but the righteous give and do not hold back.
text.Rotherham: All the day, he greatly craveth, whereas, the righteous, giveth, and doth not spare.
text.KJV: He coveteth greedily all the day long: but the righteous giveth and spareth not.
text.NABRE: Some are consumed with avarice all the day, but the just give unsparingly.
text.DRB: all the day he longeth and desireth: but he that is just, will give, and will not cease.

text.NRSV: The sacrifice of the wicked is an abomination; how much more when brought with evil intent.
text.Rotherham: The sacrifice of the lawless, is an abomination, how much more, when, with wickedness, he bringeth it in!
text.KJV: The sacrifice of the wicked is abomination: how much more, when he bringeth it with a wicked mind?
text.NABRE: The sacrifice of the wicked is an abomination, the more so when they offer it with bad intent.
text.DRB: The hosts of the impious abominable, because they are offered of wickedness.

text.NRSV: A false witness will perish, but a good listener will testify successfully.
text.Rotherham: A false witness, shall perish, but, the man who hearkeneth, with abiding effect, shall speak.
text.KJV: A false witness shall perish: but the man that heareth speaketh constantly.
text.NABRE: The false witness will perish, but one who listens will give lasting testimony.
text.DRB: Alying witness shall perish: an obedient man shall speak victory.

text.NRSV: The wicked put on a bold face, but the upright give thought to their ways.
text.Rotherham: A lawless man emboldeneth his face, but, as for the upright, he, directeth his ways.
text.KJV: A wicked man hardeneth his face: but as for the upright, he directeth his way.
text.NABRE: The face of the wicked hardens, but the upright maintains a straight course.
text.DRB: The impious man malapertly hardeneth his countenance: but he that is righteous, correcteth his way.

text.NRSV: No wisdom, no understanding, no counsel, can avail against the Lord.
text.Rotherham: There is no wisdom, nor understanding, nor counsel, to confront Yahweh.
text.KJV: There is no wisdom nor understanding nor counsel against the Lord.
text.NABRE: No wisdom, no understanding, no counsel prevail against the Lord.
text.DRB: There is no wisdom, there is no prudence, there is no counsel against our Lord.

text.NRSV: The horse is made ready for the day of battle, but the victory belongs to the Lord.
text.Rotherham: The horse, is prepared for the day of battle, but, to Yahweh, pertaineth the victory.
text.KJV: The horse is prepared against the day of battle: but safety is of the Lord.
text.NABRE: The horse is equipped for the day of battle, but victory is the Lord ’s.
text.DRB: The horse is prepared to the day of battle: but our Lord giveth salvation.

text.NRSV: A good name is to be chosen rather than great riches, and favor is better than silver or gold.
text.Rotherham: More choice, is a name, than great riches, beyond silver and gold, is pleasant grace.
text.KJV: A good name is rather to be chosen than great riches, and loving favour rather than silver and gold.
text.NABRE: Chapter 22 A good name is more desirable than great riches, and high esteem, than gold and silver.
text.DRB: Better is a good name, than much riches: above silver and gold, good grace.

text.NRSV: The rich and the poor have this in common: the Lord is the maker of them all.
text.Rotherham: The rich and the poor, meet together, the maker of them all, is Yahweh.
text.KJV: The rich and poor meet together: the Lord is the maker of them all.
text.NABRE: Rich and poor have a common bond: the Lord is the maker of them all.
text.DRB: The rich and poor have met one another: our Lord is the maker of both.

text.NRSV: The clever see danger and hide; but the simple go on, and suffer for it.
text.Rotherham: A prudent man, seeth calamity, and hideth himself, but, the simple, pass on, and suffer.
text.KJV: A prudent man foreseeth the evil, and hideth himself: but the simple pass on, and are punished.
text.NABRE: The astute see an evil and hide, while the naive continue on and pay the penalty.
text.DRB: The subtle saw evil, and hid himself: the innocent passed by, and was afflicted with damage.

text.NRSV: The reward for humility and fear of the Lord is riches and honor and life.
text.Rotherham: The reward of humility, is the reverence of Yahweh, riches, and honour, and life.
text.KJV: By humility and the fear of the Lord are riches, and honour, and life.
text.NABRE: The result of humility and fear of the Lord is riches, honor and life.
text.DRB: The end of modesty the fear of our Lord, riches and glory and life.

text.NRSV: Thorns and snares are in the way of the crooked; those who would keep their lives will keep far from them.
text.Rotherham: Thorns and snares, are in the way of the perverse,—he that guardeth his soul, shall be far from them.
text.KJV: Thorns and snares are in the way of the froward: he that doth keep his soul shall be far from them.
text.NABRE: Thorns and snares are on the path of the crooked; those who would safeguard their lives will avoid them.
text.DRB: Armour and swords in the way of the perverse: but the keeper of his own soul departeth far from them.

text.NRSV: Train children in the right way, and when old, they will not stray.
text.Rotherham: Train up a youth, in the direction of his duty, even when he becometh old, he will not depart from it.
text.KJV: Train up a child in the way he should go: and when he is old, he will not depart from it.
text.NABRE: Train the young in the way they should go; even when old, they will not swerve from it.
text.DRB: It is a proverb: A youngman according to his way, when he is old will not depart from it.

text.NRSV: The rich rule over the poor, and the borrower is the slave of the lender.
text.Rotherham: The rich, over the poor, beareth rule,—and, slave, is the borrower to the lender.
text.KJV: The rich ruleth over the poor, and the borrower is servant to the lender.
text.NABRE: The rich rule over the poor, and the borrower is the slave of the lender.
text.DRB: The richman ruleth over the poor: and he that borroweth is the servant of him that lendeth.

text.NRSV: Whoever sows injustice will reap calamity, and the rod of anger will fail.
text.Rotherham: He that soweth perversity, shall reap trouble, and, the rod of his wrath, shall be ready.
text.KJV: He that soweth iniquity shall reap vanity: and the rod of his anger shall fail.
text.NABRE: Those who sow iniquity reap calamity, and the rod used in anger will fail.
text.DRB: He that soweth iniquity, shall reap evils, and with the rod of his wrath, he shall be consumed.

text.NRSV: Those who are generous are blessed, for they share their bread with the poor.
text.Rotherham: A benevolent eye, the same, shall be blessed,—because he hath given of his bread to the poor.
text.KJV: He that hath a bountiful eye shall be blessed; for he giveth of his bread to the poor.
text.NABRE: The generous will be blessed, for they share their food with the poor.
text.DRB: He that is prone to mercy shall be blessed: for of his breads he hath given to the poor. He that giveth gifts shall purchase victory and honour: but he that receiveth taketh away the soul of the giver.

text.NRSV: Drive out a scoffer, and strife goes out; quarreling and abuse will cease.
text.Rotherham: Drive away the scoffer, that strife may depart, and quarrel, and contempt may cease.
text.KJV: Cast out the scorner, and contention shall go out; yea, strife and reproach shall cease.
text.NABRE: Expel the arrogant and discord goes too; strife and insult cease.
text.DRB: Cast out the scorner, and brawling shall go forth with him, and cause shall cease and contumelies.

text.NRSV: Those who love a pure heart and are gracious in speech will have the king as a friend.
text.Rotherham: Yahweh loveth the pure in heart, gracious are his lips, his friend is the king!
text.KJV: He that loveth pureness of heart, for the grace of his lips the king shall be his friend.
text.NABRE: The Lord loves the pure of heart; the person of winning speech has a king for a friend.
text.DRB: He that loveth cleanness of heart, for the grace of his lips, shall have the king his friend.

text.NRSV: The eyes of the Lord keep watch over knowledge, but he overthrows the words of the faithless.
text.Rotherham: The eyes of Yahweh, watch over knowledge, therefore hath he overturned the words of the treacherous.
text.KJV: The eyes of the Lord preserve knowledge, and he overthroweth the words of the transgressor.
text.NABRE: The eyes of the Lord watch over the knowledgeable, but he defeats the projects of the faithless.
text.DRB: The eyes of our Lord keep knowledge: and the words of the just are supplanted.

text.NRSV: The lazy person says, “There is a lion outside! I shall be killed in the streets!”
text.Rotherham: Saith the sluggard, A lion outside! Amidst the broadways, shall I be slain.
text.KJV: The slothful man saith, There is a lion without, I shall be slain in the streets.
text.NABRE: The sluggard says, “A lion is outside; I might be slain in the street.”
text.DRB: The slothful saith: A lion is without, in the midst of the streets I am to be slain.

text.NRSV: The mouth of a loose woman is a deep pit; he with whom the Lord is angry falls into it.
text.Rotherham: A deep chasm, is the mouth of strange women, he with whom Yahweh is indignant, falleth there.
text.KJV: The mouth of strange women is a deep pit: he that is abhorred of the Lord shall fall therein.
text.NABRE: The mouth of the foreign woman is a deep pit; whoever incurs the Lord ’s anger will fall into it.
text.DRB: Adeep pit the mouth of a strange woman: he with whom our Lord is angry, shall fall into it.

text.NRSV: Folly is bound up in the heart of a boy, but the rod of discipline drives it far from him.
text.Rotherham: Foolishness, is bound up in the heart of a youth, the rod of correction, shall drive it far from him.
text.KJV: Foolishness is bound in the heart of a child; but the rod of correction shall drive it far from him.
text.NABRE: Folly is bound to the heart of a youth, but the rod of discipline will drive it out.
text.DRB: Folly is tied together in the heart of a child, and the rod of discipline shall drive it away.

text.NRSV: Oppressing the poor in order to enrich oneself, and giving to the rich, will lead only to loss.
text.Rotherham: He that oppresseth the poor, to make increase for himself, giving to the rich, shall surely come to want.
text.KJV: He that oppresseth the poor to increase his riches, and he that giveth to the rich, shall surely come to want.
text.NABRE: Oppressing the poor for enrichment, giving to the rich: both are sheer loss.
text.DRB: He that doth calumniate the poor to increase his riches, himself shall give to a richer, and shall be in need.

text.NRSV: The words of the wise: Incline your ear and hear my words, and apply your mind to my teaching;
text.Rotherham: Incline thine ear and hear the words of wise men, then, thy heart, wilt thou apply to my teaching;
text.KJV: Bow down thine ear, and hear the words of the wise, and apply thine heart unto my knowledge.
text.NABRE: IV. Sayings of the Wise The Words of the Wise: Incline your ear, and hear my words, and let your mind attend to my teaching;
text.DRB: Incline thine ear, and hear the words of wisemen: and set thy heart to my doctrine:

text.NRSV: for it will be pleasant if you keep them within you, if all of them are ready on your lips.
text.Rotherham: For sweet shall they be, when thou shalt keep them in thine inmost mind, they shall fit well together, upon thy lips.
text.KJV: For it is a pleasant thing if thou keep them within thee; they shall withal be fitted in thy lips.
text.NABRE: For it will be well if you hold them within you, if they all are ready on your lips.
text.DRB: which shall be beautiful for thee, when thou shalt keep it in thy belly, and it shall flow in thy lips.

text.NRSV: So that your trust may be in the Lord, I have made them known to you today—yes, to you.
text.Rotherham: That in Yahweh may be thy trust, I have made them known to thee to-day, even to thee.
text.KJV: That thy trust may be in the Lord, I have made known to thee this day, even to thee.
text.NABRE: That your trust may be in the Lord, I make them known to you today—yes, to you.
text.DRB: That thy confidence may be in our Lord, wherefore I have shewed also it to thee this day.

text.NRSV: Have I not written for you thirty sayings of admonition and knowledge,
text.Rotherham: Have I not written for thee noble things, with counsels and knowledge:
text.KJV: Have not I written to thee excellent things in counsels and knowledge,
text.NABRE: Have I not written for you thirty sayings, containing counsels and knowledge,
text.DRB: Behold I have described it to thee three manner of ways: in cogitations and knowledge:

text.NRSV: to show you what is right and true, so that you may give a true answer to those who sent you?
text.Rotherham: To cause thee to know the meaning of faithful sayings, that thou mayest give back faithful sayings to them who ask thee.
text.KJV: That I might make thee know the certainty of the words of truth; that thou mightest answer the words of truth to them that send unto thee?
text.NABRE: To teach you truly how to give a dependable report to one who sends you?
text.DRB: that I might shew thee the stability, and the words of truth, out of these to answer them that sent thee.

text.NRSV: Do not rob the poor because they are poor, or crush the afflicted at the gate;
text.Rotherham: Do not rob the poor, because he is poor, neither crush thou the oppressed in the gate;
text.KJV: Rob not the poor, because he is poor: neither oppress the afflicted in the gate:
text.NABRE: Do not rob the poor because they are poor, nor crush the needy at the gate;
text.DRB: Do not violence to the poor, because he is poor: neither oppress the needy in the gate:

text.NRSV: for the Lord will plead their cause and despoil of life those who despoil them.
text.Rotherham: For, Yahweh, will plead their cause, and will despoil their despoilers of life.
text.KJV: For the Lord will plead their cause, and spoil the soul of those that spoiled them.
text.NABRE: For the Lord will defend their cause, and will plunder those who plunder them.
text.DRB: because our Lord will judge his cause, and will pierce them, that have pierced his soul.

text.NRSV: Make no friends with those given to anger, and do not associate with hotheads,
text.Rotherham: Do not have friendship with one given to anger, and, with a wrathful man, shall thou not enter in:
text.KJV: Make no friendship with an angry man; and with a furious man thou shalt not go:
text.NABRE: Do not be friendly with hotheads, nor associate with the wrathful,
text.DRB: Be not friend to an angry man, nor walk with a furious man:

text.NRSV: or you may learn their ways and entangle yourself in a snare.
text.Rotherham: Lest thou learn his ways, and take a snare to thy soul.
text.KJV: Lest thou learn his ways, and get a snare to thy soul.
text.NABRE: Lest you learn their ways, and become ensnared.
text.DRB: lest perhaps thou learn his paths, and take scandal to thy soul.

text.NRSV: Do not be one of those who give pledges, who become surety for debts.
text.Rotherham: Be not of them who strike hands, of them who are sureties for debts:
text.KJV: Be not thou one of them that strike hands, or of them that are sureties for debts.
text.NABRE: Do not be one of those who give their hand in pledge, those who become surety for debts;
text.DRB: Be not with them that stick down their hands, and that offer themselves sureties for debts:

text.NRSV: If you have nothing with which to pay, why should your bed be taken from under you?
text.Rotherham: If thou hast nothing to pay, why should one take away thy bed from under thee?
text.KJV: If thou hast nothing to pay, why should he take away thy bed from under thee?
text.NABRE: For if you are unable to pay, your bed will be taken from under you.
text.DRB: for if thou have not wherewith to restore, what cause is there that he should take the covering from thy bed?

text.NRSV: Do not remove the ancient landmark that your ancestors set up.
text.Rotherham: Do not move back the ancient boundary, which thy father’s made.
text.KJV: Remove not the ancient landmark, which thy fathers have set.
text.NABRE: Do not remove the ancient landmark that your ancestors set up.
text.DRB: Transgress not the ancient bounds, which thy fathers have put.

text.NRSV: Do you see those who are skillful in their work? They will serve kings; they will not serve common people.
text.Rotherham: Seest thou a man prompt in his business? before kings, shall he stand, he shall not stand before men who are obscure.
text.KJV: Seest thou a man diligent in his business? he shall stand before kings; he shall not stand before mean men.
text.NABRE: Do you see those skilled at their work? They will stand in the presence of kings, but not in the presence of the obscure.
text.DRB: Hast thou seen a man quick in his work? He shall stand before kings, neither shall he be before the unnoble.

text.NRSV: When you sit down to eat with a ruler, observe carefully what is before you,
text.Rotherham: When thou sittest to eat with a ruler, thou shalt consider well, what is before thee;
text.KJV: When thou sittest to eat with a ruler, consider diligently what is before thee:
text.NABRE: Chapter 23 When you sit down to dine with a ruler, mark well the one who is before you;
text.DRB: When thou shalt sit to eat with a Prince, attend diligently what things are set before thy face:

text.NRSV: and put a knife to your throat if you have a big appetite.
text.Rotherham: And shalt put a knife to thy throat, if, of great appetite, thou art:
text.KJV: And put a knife to thy throat, if thou be a man given to appetite.
text.NABRE: Stick the knife in your gullet if you have a ravenous appetite.
text.DRB: and set a knife in thy throat, if notwithstanding thou have thy soul in thine own power.

text.NRSV: Do not desire the ruler’s delicacies, for they are deceptive food.
text.Rotherham: Do not crave his dainties, for, the same, are deceitful food.
text.KJV: Be not desirous of his dainties: for they are deceitful meat.
text.NABRE: Do not desire his delicacies; it is food that deceives.
text.DRB: Desire not his meats, in which is the breath of lying.

text.NRSV: Do not wear yourself out to get rich; be wise enough to desist.
text.Rotherham: Do not toil to get wealth, of thine own understanding, forbear:
text.KJV: Labour not to be rich: cease from thine own wisdom.
text.NABRE: Do not wear yourself out to gain wealth, cease to be worried about it;
text.DRB: Labour not to be rich: but set a mean to thy prudence.

text.NRSV: When your eyes light upon it, it is gone; for suddenly it takes wings to itself, flying like an eagle toward heaven.
text.Rotherham: Wilt thou let thine eye fly thereupon, when it is nothing? for it will, surely make, itself wings, Like an eagle, will it wing its way across the heavens.
text.KJV: Wilt thou set thine eyes upon that which is not? for riches certainly make themselves wings; they fly away as an eagle toward heaven.
text.NABRE: When your glance flits to it, it is gone! For assuredly it grows wings, like the eagle that flies toward heaven.
text.DRB: Lift not up thine eyes to the riches, which thou canst not have: because they shall make to themselves wings as of an eagle, and shall fly into heaven.

text.NRSV: Do not eat the food of the begrudging, or desire their delicacies;
text.Rotherham: Do not eat the food of him that hath a begrudging eye, neither crave thou his dainties;
text.KJV: Eat thou not the bread of him that hath an evil eye, neither desire thou his dainty meats:
text.NABRE: Do not take food with unwilling hosts, and do not desire their delicacies;
text.DRB: Eat not with an envious man, and desire not his meats.

text.NRSV: for they are like a hair in the throat. “Eat and drink!” they say to you; but they do not mean it.
text.Rotherham: For, just as he hath thought in his own mind, so, he is: Eat and drink! he may say to thee, but, his heart, is not with thee.
text.KJV: For as he thinketh in his heart, so is he: Eat and drink, saith he to thee; but his heart is not with thee.
text.NABRE: For like something stuck in the throat is that food. “Eat and drink,” they say to you, but their hearts are not with you;
text.DRB: Because after the similitude of a soothsayer, and diviner, he esteemeth that which he knoweth not. Eat and drink, will he say to thee: and his mind is not with thee.

text.NRSV: You will vomit up the morsels you have eaten, and waste your pleasant words.
text.Rotherham: As for thy morsel thou hast eaten, thou shalt vomit it, so shalt thou waste thy things so sweet.
text.KJV: The morsel which thou hast eaten shalt thou vomit up, and lose thy sweet words.
text.NABRE: The little you have eaten you will vomit up, and you will have wasted your agreeable words.
text.DRB: The meats which thou hadst eaten thou shalt vomit up: and shalt lose thy beautiful words.

text.NRSV: Do not speak in the hearing of a fool, who will only despise the wisdom of your words.
text.Rotherham: In the ears of a dullard, do not speak, for he will despise the good sense of thy words.
text.KJV: Speak not in the ears of a fool: for he will despise the wisdom of thy words.
text.NABRE: Do not speak in the hearing of fools; they will despise the wisdom of your words.
text.DRB: Speak not in the ears of the unwise: because they will despise the doctrine of thy speech.

text.NRSV: Do not remove an ancient landmark or encroach on the fields of orphans,
text.Rotherham: Do not move back the ancient boundary, and, into the fields of the fatherless, do not enter;
text.KJV: Remove not the old landmark; and enter not into the fields of the fatherless:
text.NABRE: Do not remove the ancient landmark, nor invade the fields of the fatherless;
text.DRB: Touch not the bounds of little ones: and into the field of pupils enter not.

text.NRSV: for their Redeemer is strong; he will plead their cause against you.
text.Rotherham: For, their near of kin, is strong, he, will plead their cause with thee.
text.KJV: For their redeemer is mighty; he shall plead their cause with thee.
text.NABRE: For their redeemer is strong; he will defend their cause against you.
text.DRB: For their near kinsman is strong: and he will judge their cause against thee.

text.NRSV: Apply your mind to instruction and your ear to words of knowledge.
text.Rotherham: Bring, to correction, thy heart, and thine ears, to the sayings of knowledge.
text.KJV: Apply thine heart unto instruction, and thine ears to the words of knowledge.
text.NABRE: Apply your heart to instruction, and your ear to words of knowledge.
text.DRB: Let thy heart enter into doctrine: and thine ears to words of knowledge.

text.NRSV: Do not withhold discipline from your children; if you beat them with a rod, they will not die.
text.Rotherham: Do not withhold, from a child, correction, When thou smitest him with the rod, he shall not die:
text.KJV: Withhold not correction from the child: for if thou beatest him with the rod, he shall not die.
