text.NABRE: Do not withhold discipline from youths; if you beat them with the rod, they will not die.
text.DRB: Withdraw not discipline from a child: for if thou shalt strike him with the rod, he shall not die.

text.NRSV: If you beat them with the rod, you will save their lives from Sheol.
text.Rotherham: Thou, with the rod, shalt smite him, and, his soul from hades, shalt thou deliver.
text.KJV: Thou shalt beat him with the rod, and shalt deliver his soul from hell.
text.NABRE: Beat them with the rod, and you will save them from Sheol.
text.DRB: Thou shalt strike him with the rod: and deliver his soul from hell.

text.NRSV: My child, if your heart is wise, my heart also will be glad.
text.Rotherham: My son! if thy heart be wise, my heart shall rejoice, even mine.
text.KJV: My son, if thine heart be wise, my heart shall rejoice, even mine.
text.NABRE: My son, if your heart is wise, my heart also will rejoice;
text.DRB: My son, if thy mind shall be wise, my heart shall be glad with thee:

text.NRSV: My soul will rejoice when your lips speak what is right.
text.Rotherham: So shall my reins exult, when thy lips speak the things that are right.
text.KJV: Yea, my reins shall rejoice, when thy lips speak right things.
text.NABRE: And my inmost being will exult, when your lips speak what is right.
text.DRB: And my reins shall rejoice, when thy lips shall speak right things.

text.NRSV: Do not let your heart envy sinners, but always continue in the fear of the Lord.
text.Rotherham: Let not thy heart be envious of sinners, only of the reverence of Yahweh, all day long;
text.KJV: Let not thine heart envy sinners: but be thou in the fear of the Lord all the day long.
text.NABRE: Do not let your heart envy sinners, but only those who always fear the Lord;
text.DRB: Let not thy heart envy sinners: but in the fear of our Lord be thou all the day:

text.NRSV: Surely there is a future, and your hope will not be cut off.
text.Rotherham: For surely there is a future, and, thine expectation, shall not be cut off.
text.KJV: For surely there is an end; and thine expectation shall not be cut off.
text.NABRE: For you will surely have a future, and your hope will not be cut off.
text.DRB: because thou shalt have hope in the later end, and thine exaltation shall not be taken away.

text.NRSV: Hear, my child, and be wise, and direct your mind in the way.
text.Rotherham: Hear, thou, my son, and be wise, and lead forward, in duty, thy heart.
text.KJV: Hear thou, my son, and be wise, and guide thine heart in the way.
text.NABRE: Hear, my son, and be wise, and guide your heart in the right way.
text.DRB: Hear my son, & be wise: & direct thy mind in the way.

text.NRSV: Do not be among winebibbers, or among gluttonous eaters of meat;
text.Rotherham: Do not be among them who tipple with wine,—among them who are gluttons;
text.KJV: Be not among winebibbers; among riotous eaters of flesh:
text.NABRE: Do not join with wine bibbers, nor with those who glut themselves on meat.
text.DRB: Be not in the feasts of great drinkers, nor in their comessations, which contribute flesh together to eat:

text.NRSV: for the drunkard and the glutton will come to poverty, and drowsiness will clothe them with rags.
text.Rotherham: For, the tippler and the glutton, shall come to poverty, and, rags, shall Slumber put on!
text.KJV: For the drunkard and the glutton shall come to poverty: and drowsiness shall clothe a man with rags.
text.NABRE: For drunkards and gluttons come to poverty, and lazing about clothes one in rags.
text.DRB: because they that are given to drinking, and that pay shots, shall be consumed, and drowsiness shall be clothed with rags.

text.NRSV: Listen to your father who begot you, and do not despise your mother when she is old.
text.Rotherham: Hearken to thy father here, who begat thee, and despise not, when she is old, thy mother.
text.KJV: Hearken unto thy father that begat thee, and despise not thy mother when she is old.
text.NABRE: Listen to your father who begot you, do not despise your mother when she is old.
text.DRB: Hear thy father, that begot thee: and contemn not thy mother when she is old.

text.NRSV: Buy truth, and do not sell it; buy wisdom, instruction, and understanding.
text.Rotherham: Truth, buy thou, but do not sell, wisdom, and correction, and understanding.
text.KJV: Buy the truth, and sell it not; also wisdom, and instruction, and understanding.
text.NABRE: Buy truth and do not sell: wisdom, instruction, understanding!
text.DRB: Buy truth, and sell not wisdom, and doctrine, and intelligence.

text.NRSV: The father of the righteous will greatly rejoice; he who begets a wise son will be glad in him.
text.Rotherham: Greatly shall exult, the father of a righteous man, and, he that begetteth a wise son, shall rejoice in him:
text.KJV: The father of the righteous shall greatly rejoice: and he that begetteth a wise child shall have joy of him.
text.NABRE: The father of a just person will exult greatly; whoever begets a wise son will rejoice in him.
text.DRB: The father of the just rejoiceth with gladness: he that hath begotten a wiseman, shall rejoice in him.

text.NRSV: Let your father and mother be glad; let her who bore you rejoice.
text.Rotherham: Rejoice shall thy father and thy mother, yea she, shall exult, who bare thee.
text.KJV: Thy father and thy mother shall be glad, and she that bare thee shall rejoice.
text.NABRE: Let your father and mother rejoice; let her who bore you exult.
text.DRB: Let thy father be glad and thy mother, and let her rejoice that bare thee.

text.NRSV: My child, give me your heart, and let your eyes observe my ways.
text.Rotherham: Oh give, my son, thy mind unto me, and let, thine eyes, observe, my ways;
text.KJV: My son, give me thine heart, and let thine eyes observe my ways.
text.NABRE: My son, give me your heart, and let your eyes keep to my ways,
text.DRB: My son give me thy heart: & let thine eyes keep my ways.

text.NRSV: For a prostitute is a deep pit; an adulteress is a narrow well.
text.Rotherham: For, a deep chasm, is the unchaste woman, and, a narrow pit, the female unknown;
text.KJV: For a whore is a deep ditch; and a strange woman is a narrow pit.
text.NABRE: For the harlot is a deep pit, and the foreign woman a narrow well;
text.DRB: For an harlot is a deep ditch: & a strange woman a narrow pit.

text.NRSV: She lies in wait like a robber and increases the number of the faithless.
text.Rotherham: Yea, she, as for prey, lieth in wait, and, the treacherous among mankind, she causeth to abound.
text.KJV: She also lieth in wait as for a prey, and increaseth the transgressors among men.
text.NABRE: Yes, she lies in wait like a robber, and increases the number of the faithless.
text.DRB: She lieth in wait in the way as a robber, and whom she shall see not circumspect, she will kill.

text.NRSV: Who has woe? Who has sorrow? Who has strife? Who has complaining? Who has wounds without cause? Who has redness of eyes?
text.Rotherham: Who hath woe? Who hath outcry of pain? Who hath contentions? Who hath complaining? Who hath needless wounds? Who hath dullness of eyes?
text.KJV: Who hath woe? who hath sorrow? who hath contentions? who hath babbling? who hath wounds without cause? who hath redness of eyes?
text.NABRE: Who scream? Who shout? Who have strife? Who have anxiety? Who have wounds for nothing? Who have bleary eyes?
text.DRB: To whom is woe? To whose father woe? To whom brawls? To whom ditches? To whom wounds without cause? To whom blood-shedding eyes?

text.NRSV: Those who linger late over wine, those who keep trying mixed wine.
text.Rotherham: They who tarry over wine, they who go in to search for mixed wine.
text.KJV: They that tarry long at the wine; they that go to seek mixed wine.
text.NABRE: Whoever linger long over wine, whoever go around quaffing wine.
text.DRB: Is it not to them that pass their time in wine, and study to drink out their cups?

text.NRSV: Do not look at wine when it is red, when it sparkles in the cup and goes down smoothly.
text.Rotherham: Do not look on wine when it becometh red, when it giveth in the cup its sparkle, glideth down smoothly.
text.KJV: Look not thou upon the wine when it is red, when it giveth his colour in the cup, when it moveth itself aright.
text.NABRE: Do not look on the wine when it is red, when it sparkles in the cup. It goes down smoothly,
text.DRB: Behold not wine when it waxeth yellow, when the colour thereof shall shine in the glass: it goeth in pleasantly,

text.NRSV: At the last it bites like a serpent, and stings like an adder.
text.Rotherham: Its after effect, is that, like a serpent, it biteth, and, like a viper, it doth sting.
text.KJV: At the last it biteth like a serpent, and stingeth like an adder.
text.NABRE: but in the end it bites like a serpent, and stings like an adder.
text.DRB: but in the end, it will bite like a snake, and as a basilisk it will pour abroad poisons.

text.NRSV: Your eyes will see strange things, and your mind utter perverse things.
text.Rotherham: Thine eyes, will see strange women, and, thy heart, will speak perverse things:
text.KJV: Thine eyes shall behold strange women, and thine heart shall utter perverse things.
text.NABRE: Your eyes behold strange sights, and your heart utters incoherent things;
text.DRB: Thine eyes shall see strange women, and thy heart shall speak perverse things.

text.NRSV: You will be like one who lies down in the midst of the sea, like one who lies on the top of a mast.
text.Rotherham: So shalt thou become, as one lying down in the heart of the sea,—or as one lying down on the top of the mastgear:
text.KJV: Yea, thou shalt be as he that lieth down in the midst of the sea, or as he that lieth upon the top of a mast.
text.NABRE: You are like one sleeping on the high seas, sprawled at the top of the mast.
text.DRB: And thou shalt be as one sleeping in the midst of the sea, and as the governor fast asleep, the stern being lost.

text.NRSV: “They struck me,” you will say, “but I was not hurt; they beat me, but I did not feel it. When shall I awake? I will seek another drink.”
text.Rotherham: They smote me—I felt no pain, They struck me down—I noticed it not,—When shall I wake up? I will go on, I will seek it, again!
text.KJV: They have stricken me, shalt thou say, and I was not sick; they have beaten me, and I felt it not: when shall I awake? I will seek it yet again.
text.NABRE: “They struck me, but it did not pain me; they beat me, but I did not feel it. When can I get up, when can I go out and get more?”
text.DRB: And thou shalt say: They have beaten me, but I was not grieved: they drew me, and I felt not: When shall I awake, and find wines again?

text.NRSV: Do not envy the wicked, nor desire to be with them;
text.Rotherham: Be not thou envious of wicked men, neither crave to be with them;
text.KJV: Be not thou envious against evil men, neither desire to be with them.
text.NABRE: Chapter 24 Do not envy the wicked, nor desire to be with them;
text.DRB: Emulate not evil men, neither desire thou to be with them:

text.NRSV: for their minds devise violence, and their lips talk of mischief.
text.Rotherham: For, violence, their heart muttereth, and, mischief, their lips do speak.
text.KJV: For their heart studieth destruction, and their lips talk of mischief.
text.NABRE: For their hearts plot violence, and their lips speak of foul play.
text.DRB: because their mind doth meditate robberies, and their lips speak deceits.

text.NRSV: By wisdom a house is built, and by understanding it is established;
text.Rotherham: In wisdom, is a house builded, and, in understanding, is it established;
text.KJV: Through wisdom is an house builded; and by understanding it is established:
text.NABRE: By wisdom a house is built, by understanding it is established;
text.DRB: By wisdom the house shall be built, and by prudence it shall be strengthened.

text.NRSV: by knowledge the rooms are filled with all precious and pleasant riches.
text.Rotherham: And, in knowledge, chambers are filled, with all acquisitions, costly and fair.
text.KJV: And by knowledge shall the chambers be filled with all precious and pleasant riches.
text.NABRE: And by knowledge its rooms are filled with every precious and pleasing possession.
text.DRB: In doctrine the cellars shall be replenished with all precious and most beautiful substance.

text.NRSV: Wise warriors are better than strong ones, and those who have knowledge than those who have might;
text.Rotherham: A wise man, is mighty, and, a man of knowledge, becometh alert in vigour.
text.KJV: A wise man is strong; yea, a man of knowledge increaseth strength.
text.NABRE: The wise are more powerful than the strong, and the learned, than the mighty,
text.DRB: Awiseman is strong: and a learned man, strong and valiant.

text.NRSV: for by wise guidance you can wage your war, and in abundance of counselors there is victory.
text.Rotherham: Surely, with concerted measures, shalt thou make for thyself war, and, success, lieth in the greatness of the counsellor.
text.KJV: For by wise counsel thou shalt make thy war: and in multitude of counsellors there is safety.
text.NABRE: For by strategy war is waged, and victory depends on many counselors.
text.DRB: Because war is managed by due ordering: & there shall be salvation where many counsels are.

text.NRSV: Wisdom is too high for fools; in the gate they do not open their mouths.
text.Rotherham: Unattainable to a foolish man, are the dictates of wisdom, in the gate, he openeth not his mouth.
text.KJV: Wisdom is too high for a fool: he openeth not his mouth in the gate.
text.NABRE: Wise words are beyond fools’ reach, in the assembly they do not open their mouth;
text.DRB: Wisdom is high for a fool, in the gate he shall not open his mouth.

text.NRSV: Whoever plans to do evil will be called a mischief-maker.
text.Rotherham: He that deviseth to do mischief, him, shall men call, a master of plots.
text.KJV: He that deviseth to do evil shall be called a mischievous person.
text.NABRE: As they calculate how to do evil, people brand them troublemakers.
text.DRB: He that thinketh to do evils, shall be called a fool.

text.NRSV: The devising of folly is sin, and the scoffer is an abomination to all.
text.Rotherham: The purpose of folly, is sin, and, an abomination to mankind, is a buffoon.
text.KJV: The thought of foolishness is sin: and the scorner is an abomination to men.
text.NABRE: The scheme of a fool gains no acceptance, the scoffer is an abomination to the community.
text.DRB: The cogitation of a fool is sin: and a detracter the abomination of men.

text.NRSV: If you falter in the day of adversity, your strength is small.
text.Rotherham: Thou hast been slothful in the day of straitness, Strait, is thy strength.
text.KJV: If thou faint in the day of adversity, thy strength is small.
text.NABRE: Did you fail in a day of adversity, did your strength fall short?
text.DRB: If thou despair being weary in the day of distress: thy strength shall be diminished.

text.NRSV: Rescue those who are being taken away to death; hold back those who are stumbling to the slaughter.
text.Rotherham: Deliver thou such as are being led forth to death, and, them who are tottering to slaughter, oh that thou wouldst hold back!
text.KJV: If thou forbear to deliver them that are drawn unto death, and those that are ready to be slain;
text.NABRE: Did you fail to rescue those who were being dragged off to death, those tottering, those near death,
text.DRB: Deliver them that are led to death: and those that are drawn to death cease not to deliver.

text.NRSV: If you say, “But we did not know this”— does not he who weighs the heart perceive it? Does not he who keeps watch over your soul know it? And will he not repay all according to their deeds?
text.Rotherham: Though thou say, Lo! we knew not this, Shall not, he that proveth hearts, himself, discern? And, he that formeth thy soul, himself, know? and bring back to a son of earth according to his deed?
text.KJV: If thou sayest, Behold, we knew it not; doth not he that pondereth the heart consider it? and he that keepeth thy soul, doth not he know it? and shall not he render to every man according to his works?
text.NABRE: because you said, “We didn’t know about it”? Surely, the Searcher of hearts knows and will repay all according to their deeds.
text.DRB: If thou say, I am not of force: he that seeth into the heart, he understandeth, and nothing deceiveth the keeper of thy soul, and he shall render to man according to his works.

text.NRSV: My child, eat honey, for it is good, and the drippings of the honeycomb are sweet to your taste.
text.Rotherham: My son, eat thou honey, because it is good,—and droppings from the comb [because they are] sweet to thy palate:
text.KJV: My son, eat thou honey, because it is good; and the honeycomb, which is sweet to thy taste:
text.NABRE: If you eat honey, my son, because it is good, if pure honey is sweet to your taste,
text.DRB: Eat honey my son, because it is good, and the honey-comb most sweet to thy throat:

text.NRSV: Know that wisdom is such to your soul; if you find it, you will have a future, and your hope will not be cut off.
text.Rotherham: Thus, take knowledge of wisdom, for thine own soul; If thou find it, then there is a future, and, thine expectation, shall not be cut off.
text.KJV: So shall the knowledge of wisdom be unto thy soul: when thou hast found it, then there shall be a reward, and thy expectation shall not be cut off.
text.NABRE: Such, you must know, is wisdom to your soul. If you find it, you will have a future, and your hope will not be cut off.
text.DRB: so also the doctrine of wisdom to thy soul: which when thou shalt find, thou shalt have hope in the later end, and thy hope shall not perish.

text.NRSV: Do not lie in wait like a wicked person against the home of the righteous; do no violence to their place of rest;
text.Rotherham: Do not lie in wait, thou lawless man, against the home of the righteous,—neither destroy thou his place of rest;
text.KJV: Lay not wait, O wicked man, against the dwelling of the righteous; spoil not his resting place:
text.NABRE: Do not lie in wait at the abode of the just, do not ravage their dwelling places;
text.DRB: Lie not in wait, nor seek impiety in the house of the just, nor spoil his rest.

text.NRSV: for though they fall seven times, they will rise again; but the wicked are overthrown by calamity.
text.Rotherham: For, seven times, may the righteous fall and yet arise, but, lawless men, shall stumble into calamity.
text.KJV: For a just man falleth seven times, and riseth up again: but the wicked shall fall into mischief.
text.NABRE: Though the just fall seven times, they rise again, but the wicked stumble from only one mishap.
text.DRB: For seven times shall the just fall, and shall rise again: but the impious shall fall into evil.

text.NRSV: Do not rejoice when your enemies fall, and do not let your heart be glad when they stumble,
text.Rotherham: When thine enemy falleth, do not thou rejoice, and, when he stumbleth, let not thy heart exult:
text.KJV: Rejoice not when thine enemy falleth, and let not thine heart be glad when he stumbleth:
text.NABRE: Do not rejoice when your enemies fall, and when they stumble, do not let your heart exult,
text.DRB: When thine enemy shall fall, be not glad, and in his ruin let not thy heart rejoice:

text.NRSV: or the Lord will see it and be displeased, and turn through his anger from them.
text.Rotherham: Lest Yahweh see it, and it be wicked in his eyes, and he turn away from him his anger.
text.KJV: Lest the Lord see it, and it displease him, and he turn away his wrath from him.
text.NABRE: Lest the Lord see it, be displeased with you, and withdraw his wrath from your enemies.
text.DRB: Lest perhaps our Lord see, and it displease him, and he take away his wrath from him.

text.NRSV: Do not fret because of evildoers. Do not envy the wicked;
text.Rotherham: Burn not with vexation against evil doers, be not envious of lawless men;
text.KJV: Fret not thyself because of evil men, neither be thou envious at the wicked;
text.NABRE: Do not be provoked at evildoers, do not envy the wicked;
text.DRB: Contend not with the most wicked, nor emulate the impious:

text.NRSV: for the evil have no future; the lamp of the wicked will go out.
text.Rotherham: For there shall be no future for the wicked, The lamp of the lawless, shall go out.
text.KJV: For there shall be no reward to the evil man; the candle of the wicked shall be put out.
text.NABRE: For the evil have no future, the lamp of the wicked will be put out.
text.DRB: because evil men have not hope of things to come, and the lamp of the impious shall be extinguished.

text.NRSV: My child, fear the Lord and the king, and do not confide in rebels;
text.Rotherham: Revere thou Yahweh, my son, and the king, and, with the fickle, have thou no fellowship;
text.KJV: My son, fear thou the Lord and the king: and meddle not with them that are given to change:
text.NABRE: My son, fear the Lord and the king; have nothing to do with those who hate them;
text.DRB: Fear our Lord, my son, and the king: & with detracters meddle not:

text.NRSV: for disaster from them will rise suddenly, and who knows the ruin that will come from them both?
text.Rotherham: For, suddenly, shall arise their calamity; and, the misfortune of their years, who knoweth?
text.KJV: For their calamity shall rise suddenly; and who knoweth the ruin of them both?
text.NABRE: For disaster will issue suddenly, and calamity from them both, who knows when?
text.DRB: because their perdition shall suddenly rise; and the ruin of both who knoweth?

text.NRSV: These also are sayings of the wise: Partiality in judging is not good.
text.Rotherham: These things also, concern the wise, To take note of faces in judgment, is not good.
text.KJV: These things also belong to the wise. It is not good to have respect of persons in judgment.
text.NABRE: V. Further Sayings of the Wise These also are Words of the Wise: To show partiality in judgment is not good.
text.DRB: These things also to the wise: to know a person in judgement is not good.

text.NRSV: Whoever says to the wicked, “You are innocent,” will be cursed by peoples, abhorred by nations;
text.Rotherham: He that saith to the lawless man, Righteous, thou art, peoples shall denounce him, populations shall curse him;
text.KJV: He that saith unto the wicked, Thou art righteous; him shall the people curse, nations shall abhor him:
text.NABRE: Whoever says to the guilty party, “You are innocent,” will be cursed by nations, scorned by peoples;
text.DRB: They that say to the impious: Thou art just; peoples shall curse them, and tribes shall detest them.

text.NRSV: but those who rebuke the wicked will have delight, and a good blessing will come upon them.
text.Rotherham: But, to reprovers, one should be pleasant, and, upon them, should come an excellent blessing:
text.KJV: But to them that rebuke him shall be delight, and a good blessing shall come upon them.
text.NABRE: But those who render just verdicts will fare well, and on them will come the blessing of prosperity.
text.DRB: They that rebuke him, shall be praised: and blessing shall come upon them.

text.NRSV: One who gives an honest answer gives a kiss on the lips.
text.Rotherham: Lips, should one kiss with one who answereth in right words.
text.KJV: Every man shall kiss his lips that giveth a right answer.
text.NABRE: An honest reply— a kiss on the lips.
text.DRB: He shall kiss the lips, who answereth right words.

text.NRSV: Prepare your work outside, get everything ready for you in the field; and after that build your house.
text.Rotherham: Prepare, in the open, thy work, and make ready, in the field, for thyself, Afterwards, shalt thou build thy house.
text.KJV: Prepare thy work without, and make it fit for thyself in the field; and afterwards build thine house.
text.NABRE: Complete your outdoor tasks, and arrange your work in the field; afterward you can build your house.
text.DRB: Prepare thy work abroad, and diligently till thy ground: that afterward thou mayst build thy house.

text.NRSV: Do not be a witness against your neighbor without cause, and do not deceive with your lips.
text.Rotherham: Do not become a needless witness against thy neighbour, so mightest thou open too wide thy lips:
text.KJV: Be not a witness against thy neighbour without cause; and deceive not with thy lips.
text.NABRE: Do not testify falsely against your neighbor and so deceive with your lips.
text.DRB: Be not witness without cause against thy neighbour: neither allure any man with thy lips.

text.NRSV: Do not say, “I will do to others as they have done to me; I will pay them back for what they have done.”
text.Rotherham: Do not say—According to what he hath done to me, so, will I do to him, I will repay every one according to his work.
text.KJV: Say not, I will do so to him as he hath done to me: I will render to the man according to his work.
text.NABRE: Do not say, “As they did to me, so will I do to them; I will repay them according to their deeds.”
text.DRB: Say not, As he hath done to me, so will I do to him: I will render to every one according to his work.

text.NRSV: I passed by the field of one who was lazy, by the vineyard of a person without sense;
text.Rotherham: By the field of the sluggard, I passed, and by the vineyard of a man lacking sense;
text.KJV: I went by the field of the slothful, and by the vineyard of the man void of understanding;
text.NABRE: I passed by the field of a sluggard, by the vineyard of one with no sense;
text.DRB: I passed by the field of a slothful man, and by the vineyard of a foolish man:

text.NRSV: and see, it was all overgrown with thorns; the ground was covered with nettles, and its stone wall was broken down.
text.Rotherham: And lo! there had come up all over it—thorns, there had covered the face thereof—thistles, and, the stone fence thereof, had been thrown down.
text.KJV: And, lo, it was all grown over with thorns, and nettles had covered the face thereof, and the stone wall thereof was broken down.
text.NABRE: It was all overgrown with thistles; its surface was covered with nettles, and its stone wall broken down.
text.DRB: and behold nettles had filled it wholly, and thorns had covered the face thereof, and the wall of stones was destroyed.

text.NRSV: Then I saw and considered it; I looked and received instruction.
text.Rotherham: So I observed it, for myself, I applied my heart, I looked—I accepted correction:
text.KJV: Then I saw, and considered it well: I looked upon it, and received instruction.
text.NABRE: As I gazed at it, I reflected; I saw and learned a lesson:
text.DRB: Which when I had seen, I laid it in my heart, and by the example I learned discipline.

text.NRSV: A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest,
text.Rotherham: A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest:
text.KJV: Yet a little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to sleep:
text.NABRE: A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the arms to rest—
text.DRB: Alittle I say, thou shalt sleep, a little thou shalt slumber, a little shalt thou join thy hands together to rest:

text.NRSV: and poverty will come upon you like a robber, and want like an armed warrior.
text.Rotherham: So shall come in, as a highwayman, thy poverty, and, thy want, as one armed with a shield.
text.KJV: So shall thy poverty come as one that travelleth; and thy want as an armed man.
text.NABRE: Then poverty will come upon you like a robber, and want like a brigand.
text.DRB: and as a post, poverty shall come to thee, & beggary as a man armed.

text.NRSV: These are other proverbs of Solomon that the officials of King Hezekiah of Judah copied.
text.Rotherham: These also, are proverbs of Solomon,—which the men of Hezekiah king of Judah transcribed.
text.KJV: These are also proverbs of Solomon, which the men of Hezekiah king of Judah copied out.
text.NABRE: VI. Second Solomonic Collection, Collected Under King Hezekiah Chapter 25 These also are proverbs of Solomon. The servants of Hezekiah, king of Judah, transmitted them.
text.DRB: These also are the parables of Salomon, which the men of Ezechias king of Juda wrote out.

text.NRSV: It is the glory of God to conceal things, but the glory of kings is to search things out.
text.Rotherham: The glory of God, is to conceal a thing, but, the glory of kings, is to search out a thing.
text.KJV: It is the glory of God to conceal a thing: but the honour of kings is to search out a matter.
text.NABRE: It is the glory of God to conceal a matter, and the glory of kings to fathom a matter.
text.DRB: It is the glory of God to conceal the word, and the glory of kings to search the speech.

text.NRSV: Like the heavens for height, like the earth for depth, so the mind of kings is unsearchable.
text.Rotherham: The heavens for height, and the earth for depth, but, the heart of kings, cannot be searched.
text.KJV: The heaven for height, and the earth for depth, and the heart of kings is unsearchable.
text.NABRE: Like the heavens in height, and the earth in depth, the heart of kings is unfathomable.
text.DRB: The heaven above, and the earth beneath, and the heart of kings is unscrutable.

text.NRSV: Take away the dross from the silver, and the smith has a vessel for it;
text.Rotherham: Remove the dross from the silver, and there cometh forth, to the refiner, a vessel:
text.KJV: Take away the dross from the silver, and there shall come forth a vessel for the finer.
text.NABRE: Remove the dross from silver, and it comes forth perfectly purified;
text.DRB: Take away the rust from silver, and there shall come forth a most pure vessel:

text.NRSV: take away the wicked from the presence of the king, and his throne will be established in righteousness.
text.Rotherham: Remove a lawless man from before the king, that his throne, may be established in righteousness.
text.KJV: Take away the wicked from before the king, and his throne shall be established in righteousness.
text.NABRE: Remove the wicked from the presence of the king, and his throne is made firm through justice.
text.DRB: Take away impiety from the king's countenance, and his throne shall be established with justice.

text.NRSV: Do not put yourself forward in the king’s presence or stand in the place of the great;
text.Rotherham: Do not honour thyself before a king, nor, in the place of great men, do thou stand;
text.KJV: Put not forth thyself in the presence of the king, and stand not in the place of great men:
text.NABRE: Claim no honor in the king’s presence, nor occupy the place of superiors;
text.DRB: Appear not glorious before the king, and in the place of great men stand not.

text.NRSV: for it is better to be told, “Come up here,” than to be put lower in the presence of a noble. What your eyes have seen
text.Rotherham: For better it be said to thee, Come up hither,—than that thou be put lower down before a noble, whom thine own eyes, have beheld.
text.KJV: For better it is that it be said unto thee, Come up hither; than that thou shouldest be put lower in the presence of the prince whom thine eyes have seen.
text.NABRE: For it is better to be told, “Come up closer!” than to be humbled before the prince.
text.DRB: For it is better that it be said to thee: Come up hither, than that thou be humbled before the Prince.

text.NRSV: do not hastily bring into court; for what will you do in the end, when your neighbor puts you to shame?
text.Rotherham: Do not go forth to strive in haste,—lest [thou know not] what to do in the latter end thereof, when thy neighbour, hath put thee to shame.
text.KJV: Go not forth hastily to strive, lest thou know not what to do in the end thereof, when thy neighbour hath put thee to shame.
text.NABRE: What your eyes have seen do not bring forth too quickly against an opponent; For what will you do later on when your neighbor puts you to shame?
text.DRB: The things which thy eyes have seen, utter not quickly in a brawl: lest afterward thou canst not amend it, when thou hast dishonoured thy friend.

text.NRSV: Argue your case with your neighbor directly, and do not disclose another’s secret;
text.Rotherham: Thy contention, urge thou with thy neighbour, and, the secret of another, do not reveal:
text.KJV: Debate thy cause with thy neighbour himself; and discover not a secret to another:
text.NABRE: Argue your own case with your neighbor, but the secrets of others do not disclose;
text.DRB: Treat thy cause with thy friend, and reveal not a secret to a stranger:

text.NRSV: or he who hears you will bring shame upon you, and your ill repute will have no end.
text.Rotherham: Lest he that heareth expose thee, and, the report concerning thee, turn not away.
text.KJV: Lest he that heareth it put thee to shame, and thine infamy turn not away.
text.NABRE: Lest, hearing it, they reproach you, and your ill repute never ceases.
text.DRB: lest perhaps he insult against thee when he heareth, and cease not to upbraid thee. Grace and friendship deliver : which keep to thyself, lest thou become reproachful.

text.NRSV: A word fitly spoken is like apples of gold in a setting of silver.
text.Rotherham: Golden fruit in figured silver baskets, is a word spoken on fitting occasion.
text.KJV: A word fitly spoken is like apples of gold in pictures of silver.
text.NABRE: Golden apples in silver settings are words spoken at the proper time.
text.DRB: Apples of gold in silver beds, he that speaketh a word in his time.

text.NRSV: Like a gold ring or an ornament of gold is a wise rebuke to a listening ear.
text.Rotherham: A ring of gold, and a vessel of precious metal, is a wise reprover, on a hearing ear.
text.KJV: As an earring of gold, and an ornament of fine gold, so is a wise reprover upon an obedient ear.
text.NABRE: A golden earring or a necklace of fine gold— one who gives wise reproof to a listening ear.
text.DRB: Agolden earlet, and a shining precious stone, he that rebuketh a wiseman, and an obedient ear.

text.NRSV: Like the cold of snow in the time of harvest are faithful messengers to those who send them; they refresh the spirit of their masters.
text.Rotherham: As the cold of snow in the day of harvest, is a faithful messenger to them who send him,—when, the life of his masters, he restoreth.
text.KJV: As the cold of snow in the time of harvest, so is a faithful messenger to them that send him: for he refresheth the soul of his masters.
text.NABRE: Like the coolness of snow in the heat of the harvest are faithful messengers for those who send them, lifting the spirits of their masters.
text.DRB: As the cold of snow in the day of harvest, so a faithful legate to him that sent him, maketh his soul to rest.

text.NRSV: Like clouds and wind without rain is one who boasts of a gift never given.
text.Rotherham: Clouds and wind, when rain there is none, is the man who boasteth himself of a pretended gift.
text.KJV: Whoso boasteth himself of a false gift is like clouds and wind without rain.
text.NABRE: Clouds and wind but no rain— the one who boasts of a gift not given.
text.DRB: Clouds, and wind, and no rain following, a glorious man, and not accomplishing his promises.

text.NRSV: With patience a ruler may be persuaded, and a soft tongue can break a bone.
text.Rotherham: By long patience, is a judge persuaded, and, a soft tongue, breaketh the bone.
text.KJV: By long forbearing is a prince persuaded, and a soft tongue breaketh the bone.
text.NABRE: By patience is a ruler persuaded, and a soft tongue can break a bone.
text.DRB: By patience the Prince shall be pacified, and a soft tongue shall break hardness.

text.NRSV: If you have found honey, eat only enough for you, or else, having too much, you will vomit it.
text.Rotherham: Honey having found, eat to suffice thee, lest thou loathe it, and vomit it forth.
text.KJV: Hast thou found honey? eat so much as is sufficient for thee, lest thou be filled therewith, and vomit it.
text.NABRE: If you find honey, eat only what you need, lest you have your fill and vomit it up.
text.DRB: Thou hast found honey, eat that which sufficeth thee, lest perhaps being filled thou vomit it up.

text.NRSV: Let your foot be seldom in your neighbor’s house, otherwise the neighbor will become weary of you and hate you.
text.Rotherham: Withhold thy foot from the house of thy neighbour,—lest he be weary of thee, and hate thee.
text.KJV: Withdraw thy foot from thy neighbour’s house; lest he be weary of thee, and so hate thee.
text.NABRE: Let your foot be seldom in your neighbors’ house, lest they have their fill of you—and hate you.
text.DRB: Withdraw thy foot from the house of thy neighbour, lest sometime having his fill he hate thee.

text.NRSV: Like a war club, a sword, or a sharp arrow is one who bears false witness against a neighbor.
text.Rotherham: A hammer and a sword, and a sharpened arrow, is a man becoming a false witness against his neighbour.
text.KJV: A man that beareth false witness against his neighbour is a maul, and a sword, and a sharp arrow.
text.NABRE: A club, sword, or sharp arrow— the one who bears false witness against a neighbor.
text.DRB: Adart, and sword, and a sharp arrow, a man that speaketh false testimony against his neighbour.

text.NRSV: Like a bad tooth or a lame foot is trust in a faithless person in time of trouble.
text.Rotherham: A broken tooth and a faltering foot, is confidence in the treacherous, in the day of danger.
text.KJV: Confidence in an unfaithful man in time of trouble is like a broken tooth, and a foot out of joint.
text.NABRE: A bad tooth or an unsteady foot— a trust betrayed in time of trouble.
text.DRB: Arotten tooth, and weary foot, he that hopeth upon the unfaithful in the day of distress,

text.NRSV: Like one who takes off a garment on a cold day, and like vinegar on a wound, is one who sings songs to a heavy heart. Like a moth in clothing or a worm in wood, sorrow gnaws at the human heart.
text.Rotherham: As splendour of dress on a cold day—vinegar upon nitre, so is a singer with songs, unto a sad heart.
text.KJV: As he that taketh away a garment in cold weather, and as vinegar upon nitre, so is he that singeth songs to an heavy heart.
text.NABRE: Like the removal of clothes on a cold day, or vinegar on soda, is the one who sings to a troubled heart.
text.DRB: and that loseth his cloak in the day of cold. Vinegar in nitre, he that singeth songs to a naughty heart. As a moth the garment, and a worm the wood: so the sadness of a man hurteth the heart.

text.NRSV: If your enemies are hungry, give them food to eat; and if they are thirsty, give them water to drink;
text.Rotherham: If he that hateth thee hunger, give him bread to eat, and, if he be thirsty, give him water to drink;
text.KJV: If thine enemy be hungry, give him bread to eat; and if he be thirsty, give him water to drink:
text.NABRE: If your enemies are hungry, give them food to eat, if thirsty, give something to drink;
text.DRB: If thine enemy shall hunger, give him meat: if he thirst, give him water to drink:

text.NRSV: for you will heap burning coals on their heads, and the Lord will reward you.
text.Rotherham: For, burning coals, shalt thou be heaping upon his head,—and, Yahweh, will repay thee.
text.KJV: For thou shalt heap coals of fire upon his head, and the Lord shall reward thee.
text.NABRE: For live coals you will heap on their heads, and the Lord will vindicate you.
text.DRB: for thou shalt heap hot coals upon his head, and our Lord will reward thee.

text.NRSV: The north wind produces rain, and a backbiting tongue, angry looks.
text.Rotherham: A north wind, bringeth forth rain, and, a face stirred with indignation, a secretive tongue.
text.KJV: The north wind driveth away rain: so doth an angry countenance a backbiting tongue.
text.NABRE: The north wind brings rain, and a backbiting tongue, angry looks.
text.DRB: The north-wind dissipateth rains, & a sad look the tongue that detracteth.

text.NRSV: It is better to live in a corner of the housetop than in a share of a house with a contentious woman.
text.Rotherham: Better to dwell on the corner of the roof, than a quarrelsome wife, and a house in common.
text.KJV: It is better to dwell in the corner of the housetop, than with a brawling woman and in a wide house.
text.NABRE: It is better to dwell in a corner of the housetop than in a mansion with a quarrelsome wife.
text.DRB: It is better to sit in a corner of the house top, than with a brawling woman, and in a common house.

text.NRSV: Like cold water to a thirsty soul, so is good news from a far country.
text.Rotherham: As cold water to a thirsty soul, so is a good report from a far country.
text.KJV: As cold waters to a thirsty soul, so is good news from a far country.
text.NABRE: Cool water to one faint from thirst is good news from a far country.
text.DRB: Cold water to a thirsty soul, and good tidings from a far country.

text.NRSV: Like a muddied spring or a polluted fountain are the righteous who give way before the wicked.
text.Rotherham: A fountain fouled, a spring spoiled, is a righteous man tottering before one who is lawless.
text.KJV: A righteous man falling down before the wicked is as a troubled fountain, and a corrupt spring.
text.NABRE: A trampled fountain or a polluted spring— a just person fallen before the wicked.
text.DRB: Afountain troubled with the foot, and a vein corrupted, the just falling before the impious.

text.NRSV: It is not good to eat much honey, or to seek honor on top of honor.
text.Rotherham: To eat honey in abundance, is not good, nor is, searching out their own honour, an honourable thing.
text.KJV: It is not good to eat much honey: so for men to search their own glory is not glory.
text.NABRE: To eat too much honey is not good; nor to seek honor after honor.
text.DRB: As he that eateth much honey, it is not good for him: so he that is a searcher of the majesty, shall be oppressed of the glory.

text.NRSV: Like a city breached, without walls, is one who lacks self-control.
text.Rotherham: A city broken down without a wall, is a man who hath no control over his own spirit.
text.KJV: He that hath no rule over his own spirit is like a city that is broken down, and without walls.
text.NABRE: A city breached and left defenseless are those who do not control their temper.
text.DRB: As a city being open and without compass of walls, so a man that cannot repress his spirit in speaking.

text.NRSV: Like snow in summer or rain in harvest, so honor is not fitting for a fool.
text.Rotherham: As snow in summer, and as rain in harvest, so, unbecoming to a dullard is honour.
text.KJV: As snow in summer, and as rain in harvest, so honour is not seemly for a fool.
text.NABRE: Chapter 26 Like snow in summer, like rain in harvest, honor for a fool is out of place.
text.DRB: As snow in the summer, and rain in the harvest: so is glory undecent for a fool.

text.NRSV: Like a sparrow in its flitting, like a swallow in its flying, an undeserved curse goes nowhere.
text.Rotherham: As a sparrow in wandering, as a swallow in flying, so, a causeless curse, shall not alight.
text.KJV: As the bird by wandering, as the swallow by flying, so the curse causeless shall not come.
text.NABRE: Like the sparrow in its flitting, like the swallow in its flight, a curse uncalled-for never lands.
text.DRB: As a bird flying to other places, & a sparrow going whither he lift: so a curse uttered in vain shall light upon some man.

text.NRSV: A whip for the horse, a bridle for the donkey, and a rod for the back of fools.
text.Rotherham: A whip for the horse, a bridle for the ass, and a rod for the back of dullards.
text.KJV: A whip for the horse, a bridle for the ass, and a rod for the fool’s back.
text.NABRE: The whip for the horse, the bridle for the ass, and the rod for the back of fools.
text.DRB: Awhip for a horse, and a snaffle for an ass, and a rod on the back of the unwise.

text.NRSV: Do not answer fools according to their folly, or you will be a fool yourself.
text.Rotherham: Do not answer a dullard, according to his folly, lest, even thou thyself, become like him;
text.KJV: Answer not a fool according to his folly, lest thou also be like unto him.
text.NABRE: Do not answer fools according to their folly, lest you too become like them.
text.DRB: Answer not a fool according to his folly, lest thou be made like to him.

text.NRSV: Answer fools according to their folly, or they will be wise in their own eyes.
text.Rotherham: Answer a dullard according to his folly, lest he become wise in his own eyes.
text.KJV: Answer a fool according to his folly, lest he be wise in his own conceit.
text.NABRE: Answer fools according to their folly, lest they become wise in their own eyes.
text.DRB: Answer a fool according to his folly, lest he seem to himself to be wise.

text.NRSV: It is like cutting off one’s foot and drinking down violence, to send a message by a fool.
text.Rotherham: One who cutteth off feet, one who drinketh down wrong, is he who sendeth a message by the hand of a dullard.
text.KJV: He that sendeth a message by the hand of a fool cutteth off the feet, and drinketh damage.
text.NABRE: Those who send messages by a fool cut off their feet; they drink down violence.
text.DRB: Lame of feet, and drinking iniquity, he that sendeth words by a foolish messenger.

text.NRSV: The legs of a disabled person hang limp; so does a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
text.Rotherham: Useless are the legs of the lame, and a proverb, in the mouth of a dullard.
text.KJV: The legs of the lame are not equal: so is a parable in the mouth of fools.
text.NABRE: A proverb in the mouth of a fool hangs limp, like crippled legs.
text.DRB: As a lame man hath fair legs in vain: so a parable is undecent in the mouth of fools.

text.NRSV: It is like binding a stone in a sling to give honor to a fool.
text.Rotherham: Like tying a stone to a sling, so, is he that giveth honour, to a dullard.
text.KJV: As he that bindeth a stone in a sling, so is he that giveth honour to a fool.
text.NABRE: Giving honor to a fool is like entangling a stone in the sling.
text.DRB: As he that casteth a stone into the heap of Mercury: so he that giveth honour to the unwise.

text.NRSV: Like a thornbush brandished by the hand of a drunkard is a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
text.Rotherham: A brier cometh into the hand of a drunken-man, a proverb into the mouth of dullards.
text.KJV: As a thorn goeth up into the hand of a drunkard, so is a parable in the mouth of fools.
text.NABRE: A thorn stuck in the hand of a drunkard is a proverb in the mouth of fools.
text.DRB: As if a thorn should grow in the hand of the drunkard: so a parable in the mouth of fools.

text.NRSV: Like an archer who wounds everybody is one who hires a passing fool or drunkard.
text.Rotherham: [As] an archer who woundeth every thing, [so] one who hireth a dullard, and a drunkard crossing the sea.
text.KJV: The great God that formed all things both rewardeth the fool, and rewardeth transgressors.
text.NABRE: An archer wounding all who pass by is anyone who hires a drunken fool.
text.DRB: Judgement determineth causes: and he that putteth a fool to silence, appeaseth angers.

text.NRSV: Like a dog that returns to its vomit is a fool who reverts to his folly.
text.Rotherham: As, a dog, returneth onto his own vomit, a dullard, repeateth his folly.
text.KJV: As a dog returneth to his vomit, so a fool returneth to his folly.
text.NABRE: As dogs return to their vomit, so fools repeat their folly.
text.DRB: As a dog that returneth to his vomit, so the unwise that reiterateth his folly.

text.NRSV: Do you see persons wise in their own eyes? There is more hope for fools than for them.
text.Rotherham: Thou hast seen a man wise in his own eye,—more hope of a dullard, than of him!
text.KJV: Seest thou a man wise in his own conceit? there is more hope of a fool than of him.
text.NABRE: You see those who are wise in their own eyes? There is more hope for fools than for them.
text.DRB: Hast thou seen a man seem to himself wise? The fool shall have hope rather than he.

text.NRSV: The lazy person says, “There is a lion in the road! There is a lion in the streets!”
text.Rotherham: Saith the sluggard, A roaring lion in the road! A tearing lion in the midst of the broadways.
text.KJV: The slothful man saith, There is a lion in the way; a lion is in the streets.
text.NABRE: The sluggard says, “There is a lion in the street, a lion in the middle of the square!”
text.DRB: The slothful saith: A lion is in the way, and a lioness in the journeys:

text.NRSV: As a door turns on its hinges, so does a lazy person on a bed.
text.Rotherham: The door, turneth on its hinges, and, the sluggard, upon his bed.
text.KJV: As the door turneth upon his hinges, so doth the slothful upon his bed.
text.NABRE: The door turns on its hinges and sluggards, on their beds.
text.DRB: as a door turneth on his hinge so the slothful in his bed.

text.NRSV: The lazy person buries a hand in the dish, and is too tired to bring it back to the mouth.
text.Rotherham: The sluggard burieth his hand in the dish, he is too lazy to bring it back to his mouth.
text.KJV: The slothful hideth his hand in his bosom; it grieveth him to bring it again to his mouth.
text.NABRE: The sluggard buries a hand in the dish, too weary to lift it to the mouth.
text.DRB: The slothful hideth his hand under the arm-hole, and is grieved if he turneth it to his mouth.

text.NRSV: The lazy person is wiser in self-esteem than seven who can answer discreetly.
text.Rotherham: Wiser is the sluggard in his own eyes, than, seven persons, who can answer with judgment.
text.KJV: The sluggard is wiser in his own conceit than seven men that can render a reason.
text.NABRE: In their own eyes sluggards are wiser than seven who answer with good judgment.
text.DRB: The slothful seemeth wiser to himself, than seven men speaking sentences.

text.NRSV: Like one who seizes a passing dog by the ears is one who meddles in the quarrel of another.
text.Rotherham: As he who layeth hold of the ears of a dog, is a passer-by, who giveth vent to his wrath over a quarrel, not his!
text.KJV: He that passeth by, and meddleth with strife belonging not to him, is like one that taketh a dog by the ears.
text.NABRE: Whoever meddles in the quarrel of another is one who grabs a passing dog by the ears.
text.DRB: As he that taketh a dog by the ears, so he that passeth by impatient, and meddleth with another man's brawl.

text.NRSV: Like a maniac who shoots deadly firebrands and arrows,
text.Rotherham: As a madman throwing firebrands, arrows and death,
text.KJV: As a mad man who casteth firebrands, arrows, and death,
text.NABRE: Like a crazed archer scattering firebrands and deadly arrows,
text.DRB: As he is hurtful that shooteth arrows and spears unto death:

text.NRSV: is one who deceives a neighbor and says, “I am only joking!”
text.Rotherham: So, is a man who deceiveth his neighbour, and saith, Was not, I, in sport?
text.KJV: So is the man that deceiveth his neighbour, and saith, Am not I in sport?
text.NABRE: Such are those who deceive their neighbor, and then say, “I was only joking.”
text.DRB: so a man that hurteth his friend fraudulently: and when he is taken withal saith: I did it in jest.

text.NRSV: For lack of wood the fire goes out, and where there is no whisperer, quarreling ceases.
text.Rotherham: Without wood a fire is quenched, and, where there is no tattler, strife is hushed.
text.KJV: Where no wood is, there the fire goeth out: so where there is no talebearer, the strife ceaseth.
text.NABRE: Without wood the fire dies out; without a talebearer strife subsides.
text.DRB: When wood faileth, the fire shall be extinguished: and the whisperer taken away, brawls cease.

text.NRSV: As charcoal is to hot embers and wood to fire, so is a quarrelsome person for kindling strife.
text.Rotherham: Black coal to burning blocks, and wood to fire, so is a contentious man, for kindling strife.
text.KJV: As coals are to burning coals, and wood to fire; so is a contentious man to kindle strife.
text.NABRE: Charcoal for coals, wood for fire— such are the quarrelsome, enkindling strife.
text.DRB: As coals to burning coals, and wood to fire, so an angry man raiseth brawls.

text.NRSV: The words of a whisperer are like delicious morsels; they go down into the inner parts of the body.
text.Rotherham: the words of a tattler, are dainties, they, therefore go down into the chambers of the inner man.
text.KJV: The words of a talebearer are as wounds, and they go down into the innermost parts of the belly.
text.NABRE: The words of a talebearer are like dainty morsels: they sink into one’s inmost being.
text.DRB: The words of the whisperer as it were simple, and the same come to the inmost parts of the belly.

text.NRSV: Like the glaze covering an earthen vessel are smooth lips with an evil heart.
text.Rotherham: Dross silver overlaid upon earthenware, so are burning lips, with a mischievous heart:
text.KJV: Burning lips and a wicked heart are like a potsherd covered with silver dross.
text.NABRE: Like a glazed finish on earthenware are smooth lips and a wicked heart.
text.DRB: As if thou wouldest adorn an earthen vessel with drossy silver, so swelling lips joined with a most wicked heart.

text.NRSV: An enemy dissembles in speaking while harboring deceit within;
text.Rotherham: With his lips, the hater dissembleth, but, within himself, he layeth up deceit:
text.KJV: He that hateth dissembleth with his lips, and layeth up deceit within him;
text.NABRE: With their lips enemies pretend, but inwardly they maintain deceit;
text.DRB: An enemy is perceived by his lips, when he shall handle deceits in his heart.

text.NRSV: when an enemy speaks graciously, do not believe it, for there are seven abominations in the mind;
text.Rotherham: Though he make gracious his voice, do not trust him, for, seven abominations, are in his heart:
text.KJV: When he speaketh fair, believe him not: for there are seven abominations in his heart.
text.NABRE: When they speak graciously, do not trust them, for seven abominations are in their hearts.
text.DRB: When he shall submit his voice, believe him not: because there are seven mischiefs in his heart.

text.NRSV: though hatred be covered with guile, its wickedness will be exposed in the assembly.
text.Rotherham: Hatred may clothe itself with guile, his wickedness shall be disclosed in the convocation.
text.KJV: Whose hatred is covered by deceit, his wickedness shall be shewed before the whole congregation.
text.NABRE: Hatred can be concealed by pretense, but malice will be revealed in the assembly.
text.DRB: He that covereth hatred fraudulently, his malice shall be revealed in the councel.

text.NRSV: Whoever digs a pit will fall into it, and a stone will come back on the one who starts it rolling.
text.Rotherham: He that diggeth a pit, thereinto, shall fall, and, he that rolleth a stone, upon himself, shall it return.
text.KJV: Whoso diggeth a pit shall fall therein: and he that rolleth a stone, it will return upon him.
text.NABRE: Whoever digs a pit falls into it; and a stone comes back upon the one who rolls it.
text.DRB: He that diggeth a pit, shall fall into it: and he that rolleth a stone, it shall return to him.

text.NRSV: A lying tongue hates its victims, and a flattering mouth works ruin.
text.Rotherham: A false tongue, hateth them who are crushed by it, and, a flattering mouth, worketh occasion of stumbling.
text.KJV: A lying tongue hateth those that are afflicted by it; and a flattering mouth worketh ruin.
text.NABRE: The lying tongue is its owner’s enemy, and the flattering mouth works ruin.
text.DRB: Adeceitful tongue loveth not truth, and a slipper mouth worketh ruins.

text.NRSV: Do not boast about tomorrow, for you do not know what a day may bring.
text.Rotherham: Do not boast thyself of to-morrow, for thou knowest not what a day may bring forth.
text.KJV: Boast not thyself of to morrow; for thou knowest not what a day may bring forth.
text.NABRE: Chapter 27 Do not boast about tomorrow, for you do not know what any day may bring forth.
text.DRB: Boast not for tomorrow, being ignorant what the day to come may bring forth.

text.NRSV: Let another praise you, and not your own mouth—a stranger, and not your own lips.
text.Rotherham: Let another praise thee, and not thine own mouth, a stranger, and not thine own lips.
text.KJV: Let another man praise thee, and not thine own mouth; a stranger, and not thine own lips.
text.NABRE: Let another praise you, not your own mouth; a stranger, not your own lips.
text.DRB: Let another praise thee, and not thine own mouth: a stranger and not thine own lips.

text.NRSV: A stone is heavy, and sand is weighty, but a fool’s provocation is heavier than both.
text.Rotherham: Heavy is a stone, and weighty is sand,—but, the vexation of a fool, is heavier than both.
text.KJV: A stone is heavy, and the sand weighty; but a fool’s wrath is heavier than them both.
text.NABRE: Stone is heavy, and sand a burden, but a fool’s provocation is heavier than both.
text.DRB: Astone is heavy, and sand weighty: but the anger of a fool is heavier than both.

text.NRSV: Wrath is cruel, anger is overwhelming, but who can stand before jealousy?
text.Rotherham: The cruelty of rage, and the overflow of anger! But who can stand before, jealousy?
text.KJV: Wrath is cruel, and anger is outrageous; but who is able to stand before envy?
text.NABRE: Anger is cruel, and wrath overwhelming, but before jealousy who can stand?
text.DRB: Anger hath no mercy, nor fury breaking forth: and the violence of a moved spirit who can sustain?

text.NRSV: Better is open rebuke than hidden love.
text.Rotherham: Better is a rebuke that is open, than love carefully concealed.
text.KJV: Open rebuke is better than secret love.
text.NABRE: Better is an open rebuke than a love that remains hidden.
text.DRB: Better is manifest correption, than love hidden.

text.NRSV: Well meant are the wounds a friend inflicts, but profuse are the kisses of an enemy.
text.Rotherham: Faithful are the wounds of a friend, but lavished are the kisses of an enemy.
text.KJV: Faithful are the wounds of a friend; but the kisses of an enemy are deceitful.
text.NABRE: Trustworthy are the blows of a friend, dangerous, the kisses of an enemy.
text.DRB: Better are the wounds of him that loveth, than the fraudulent kisses of him that hateth.

text.NRSV: The sated appetite spurns honey, but to a ravenous appetite even the bitter is sweet.
text.Rotherham: The surfeited soul, trampleth upon droppings from the comb, but, to the hungry soul, every bitter thing, is sweet.
text.KJV: The full soul loatheth an honeycomb; but to the hungry soul every bitter thing is sweet.
text.NABRE: One who is full spurns honey; but to the hungry, any bitter thing is sweet.
text.DRB: Asoul that is full shall tread upon the honeycomb: and a soul that is hungry shall take bitter also for sweet.

text.NRSV: Like a bird that strays from its nest is a person who strays from home.
text.Rotherham: As a bird wandering from her nest, so, is a man wandering from his place.
text.KJV: As a bird that wandereth from her nest, so is a man that wandereth from his place.
text.NABRE: Like a bird far from the nest so is anyone far from home.
text.DRB: As a bird fleeting from her nest, so a man that forsaketh his place.

text.NRSV: Perfume and incense make the heart glad, but the soul is torn by trouble.
text.Rotherham: Oil and perfume, rejoice the heart, the sweetness of one’s friend, more than fragrant wood.
text.KJV: Ointment and perfume rejoice the heart: so doth the sweetness of a man’s friend by hearty counsel.
text.NABRE: Perfume and incense bring joy to the heart, but by grief the soul is torn asunder.
text.DRB: The heart is delighted with ointment and diverse odours: and with the good counsels of a friend the soul is sweetened.

text.NRSV: Do not forsake your friend or the friend of your parent; do not go to the house of your kindred in the day of your calamity. Better is a neighbor who is near than kindred who are far away.
text.Rotherham: Thine own friend and thy father’s friend, do not thou forsake; but, the house of thy brother, do not enter, in thy day of calamity, Better a neighbour near, than a brother far off.
text.KJV: Thine own friend, and thy father’s friend, forsake not; neither go into thy brother’s house in the day of thy calamity: for better is a neighbour that is near than a brother far off.
text.NABRE: Do not give up your own friend and your father’s friend; do not resort to the house of your kindred when trouble strikes. Better a neighbor near than kin far away.
text.DRB: Thy friend, and thy father's friend do not leave: and go not into thy brother's house in the day of thine affliction. Better is a neighbour near than a brother far off.

text.NRSV: Be wise, my child, and make my heart glad, so that I may answer my critics.
text.Rotherham: Be wise, my son, and rejoice my heart, that I may answer him that reproacheth me in a matter.
text.KJV: My son, be wise, and make my heart glad, that I may answer him that reproacheth me.
text.NABRE: Be wise, my son, and bring joy to my heart, so that I can answer whoever taunts me.
text.DRB: Study wisdom my son, & make my heart joyful, that thou mayst make answer to the upbraider.

text.NRSV: The clever see danger and hide; but the simple go on, and suffer for it.
text.Rotherham: A prudent man, seeth calamity—he hideth himself, the simple, pass on—they suffer.
text.KJV: A prudent man foreseeth the evil, and hideth himself; but the simple pass on, and are punished.
text.NABRE: The astute see an evil and hide; the naive continue on and pay the penalty.
text.DRB: The subtle man seeing evil, hideth himself: little ones passing through have sustained evil detriments.

text.NRSV: Take the garment of one who has given surety for a stranger; seize the pledge from one who has given surety for foreigners.
text.Rotherham: Take a man’s garment when he hath become pledge for a stranger, then, for a female unknown, accept him as surety.
text.KJV: Take his garment that is surety for a stranger, and take a pledge of him for a strange woman.
text.NABRE: Take the garment of the one who became surety for a stranger; if for a foreign woman, exact the pledge!
text.DRB: Take his garment that hath been surety for a stranger: and for aliens take from him a pledge.

text.NRSV: Whoever blesses a neighbor with a loud voice, rising early in the morning, will be counted as cursing.
text.Rotherham: He that blesseth his friend, with a loud voice, in the morning early, a reproach, shall it be reckoned to him.
text.KJV: He that blesseth his friend with a loud voice, rising early in the morning, it shall be counted a curse to him.
text.NABRE: Those who greet their neighbor with a loud voice in the early morning, a curse can be laid to their charge.
text.DRB: He that blesseth his neighbour with a loud voice, rising in the night, he shall be like him that curseth.

text.NRSV: A continual dripping on a rainy day and a contentious woman are alike;
text.Rotherham: A continuous dripping on a day of downpour, and a contentious wife, are alike:
text.KJV: A continual dropping in a very rainy day and a contentious woman are alike.
text.NABRE: For a persistent leak on a rainy day the match is a quarrelsome wife;
text.DRB: Roofs dropping through in the day of cold, and a brawling woman are compared together:

text.NRSV: to restrain her is to restrain the wind or to grasp oil in the right hand.
text.Rotherham: He that hideth her, hideth the wind, and, perfume, his right hand may proclaim.
text.KJV: Whosoever hideth her hideth the wind, and the ointment of his right hand, which bewrayeth itself.
text.NABRE: Whoever would hide her hides a stormwind and cannot tell north from south.
text.DRB: He that retaineth her, as he that should hold the wind, and shall call it the oil of his right hand.

text.NRSV: Iron sharpens iron, and one person sharpens the wits of another.
text.Rotherham: Let, iron, by iron, become sharp, and let, a man, sharpen the face of his friend.
text.KJV: Iron sharpeneth iron; so a man sharpeneth the countenance of his friend.
text.NABRE: Iron is sharpened by iron; one person sharpens another.
text.DRB: Iron is sharpened with iron, and a man sharpeneth the face of his friend.

text.NRSV: Anyone who tends a fig tree will eat its fruit, and anyone who renders service to a master will be honored.
text.Rotherham: He that guardeth the fig-tree, shall eat the fruit thereof, and, he that watcheth over his master, shall be honoured.
text.KJV: Whoso keepeth the fig tree shall eat the fruit thereof: so he that waiteth on his master shall be honoured.
text.NABRE: Those who tend a fig tree eat its fruit; so those attentive to their master will be honored.
text.DRB: He that keepeth the figtree, shall eat the fruits thereof: and he that is the keeper of his master, shall be glorified.

text.NRSV: As sun reflects the face, so the mind of one person reflects that of another.
text.Rotherham: As in water, face [answereth] to face, so, the heart of man to man.
text.KJV: As in water face answereth to face, so the heart of man to man.
text.NABRE: As face mirrors face in water, so the heart reflects the person.
text.DRB: As in waters the countenance of them that look therein shineth, so the hearts of men are manifest to the prudent.

text.NRSV: Sheol and Abaddon are never satisfied, and human eyes are never satisfied.
text.Rotherham: Hades and destruction, are not satisfied, and, the eyes of a man, are not satisfied.
text.KJV: Hell and destruction are never full; so the eyes of man are never satisfied.
text.NABRE: Sheol and Abaddon can never be satisfied; so the eyes of mortals can never be satisfied.
text.DRB: Hell and perdition are never filled: in like manner also the eyes of men are unsatiable.

text.NRSV: The crucible is for silver, and the furnace is for gold, so a person is tested by being praised.
text.Rotherham: Fining pot for silver, and crucible for gold, and, a man, [is to be tried] by what he praiseth.
text.KJV: As the fining pot for silver, and the furnace for gold; so is a man to his praise.
text.NABRE: The crucible for silver, the furnace for gold, so you must assay the praise you receive.
text.DRB: As silver is tried in the forge, and gold in the furnace: so a man is proved by the mouth of him that praiseth. The heart of the wicked seeketh after evils, but the righteous heart seeketh after knowledge.

text.NRSV: Crush a fool in a mortar with a pestle along with crushed grain, but the folly will not be driven out.
text.Rotherham: Though thou pound a fool in a mortar, amidst grain, with a pestle, his folly, will not depart from him.
text.KJV: Though thou shouldest bray a fool in a mortar among wheat with a pestle, yet will not his foolishness depart from him.
text.NABRE: Though you pound fools with a pestle, their folly never leaves them.
text.DRB: If thou shall bray a fool in a mortar, as when a pestle striketh upon ptisane, his folly shall not be taken from him.

text.NRSV: Know well the condition of your flocks, and give attention to your herds;
text.Rotherham: Note well, the appearance of thy flock, apply thy mind to thy herds;
text.KJV: Be thou diligent to know the state of thy flocks, and look well to thy herds.
text.NABRE: Take good care of your flocks, give careful attention to your herds;
text.DRB: Know diligently the countenance of thy cattle, and consider thy flocks:

text.NRSV: for riches do not last forever, nor a crown for all generations.
text.Rotherham: For, not age-abiding, are riches, nor is the diadem, from generation to generation:—
text.KJV: For riches are not for ever: and doth the crown endure to every generation?
text.NABRE: For wealth does not last forever, nor even a crown from age to age.
text.DRB: For thou shalt not have power always: but a crown shall be given into generation and generation.

text.NRSV: When the grass is gone, and new growth appears, and the herbage of the mountains is gathered,
text.Rotherham: The grass, is taken away, and the young shoot, showeth itself, and the herbage of the mountains, is gathered;
text.KJV: The hay appeareth, and the tender grass sheweth itself, and herbs of the mountains are gathered.
text.NABRE: When the grass comes up and the new growth appears, and the mountain greens are gathered in,
text.DRB: The meadows are open, and the green herbs have appeared, and the grass is gathered out of the mountains.

text.NRSV: the lambs will provide your clothing, and the goats the price of a field;
text.Rotherham: There are, lambs, for thy clothing, and, for the price of thy field, there are he-goats;
text.KJV: The lambs are for thy clothing, and the goats are the price of the field.
text.NABRE: The lambs will provide you with clothing, and the goats, the price of a field,
text.DRB: Lambs for thy garment: and kids the price of the field.

text.NRSV: there will be enough goats’ milk for your food, for the food of your household and maintenance for your servant-girls.
text.Rotherham: With, enough goats-milk, for thy food—for the food of thy household, and, a maintenance, for thy maidens.
text.KJV: And thou shalt have goats’ milk enough for thy food, for the food of thy household, and for the maintenance for thy maidens.
text.NABRE: And there will be ample goat’s milk for your food, food for your house, sustenance for your maidens.
text.DRB: Let the milk of the goats suffice thee for thy meats, and for the necessities of thy house: and for victual to thy hand-maids.

text.NRSV: The wicked flee when no one pursues, but the righteous are as bold as a lion.
text.Rotherham: The lawless, fleeth when no man pursueth, but, the righteous, like a lion, are confident.
text.KJV: The wicked flee when no man pursueth: but the righteous are bold as a lion.
text.NABRE: Chapter 28 The wicked flee though none pursue; but the just, like a lion, are confident.
text.DRB: The impious flyeth, no man pursuing: but the just confident as a lion, shall be without terror.

text.NRSV: When a land rebels, it has many rulers; but with an intelligent ruler there is lasting order.
text.Rotherham: For the transgressions of a land, many are the rulers thereof, but, under an intelligent and discerning man, stability is prolonged.
text.KJV: For the transgression of a land many are the princes thereof: but by a man of understanding and knowledge the state thereof shall be prolonged.
text.NABRE: If a land is rebellious, its princes will be many; but with an intelligent and wise ruler there is stability.
text.DRB: For the sins of the land the Princes thereof shall be many: & for the wisdom of a man, & the knowledge of those things that are said, the life of the Prince shall be longer.

text.NRSV: A ruler who oppresses the poor is a beating rain that leaves no food.
text.Rotherham: A poor man, who oppresseth the helpless, [is like] a rain beating down, leaving no food.
text.KJV: A poor man that oppresseth the poor is like a sweeping rain which leaveth no food.
text.NABRE: One who is poor and extorts from the lowly is a devastating rain that leaves no food.
text.DRB: Apoor man calumniating the poor, is like a vehement shower, whereby famine is gotten.

text.NRSV: Those who forsake the law praise the wicked, but those who keep the law struggle against them.
text.Rotherham: They who forsake instruction, praise one who is lawless, while, they who keep instruction, are at strife with them.
text.KJV: They that forsake the law praise the wicked: but such as keep the law contend with them.
text.NABRE: Those who abandon instruction praise the wicked, but those who keep instruction oppose them.
text.DRB: They that forsake the law, praise the impious: they that keep it, are set on fire against him.

text.NRSV: The evil do not understand justice, but those who seek the Lord understand it completely.
text.Rotherham: Wicked men, consider not justice, but, they who seek Yahweh, consider everything.
text.KJV: Evil men understand not judgment: but they that seek the Lord understand all things.
text.NABRE: The evil understand nothing of justice, but those who seek the Lord understand everything.
text.DRB: Evil men think not on judgment: but they that seek after our Lord, mark all things.

text.NRSV: Better to be poor and walk in integrity than to be crooked in one’s ways even though rich.
text.Rotherham: Better a poor man walking in his integrity, than one who is crooked—turning two ways, though, he, be rich.
text.KJV: Better is the poor that walketh in his uprightness, than he that is perverse in his ways, though he be rich.
text.NABRE: Better to be poor and walk in integrity than rich and crooked in one’s ways.
text.DRB: Better is a poor man walking in his simplicity, than the rich in crooked ways.

text.NRSV: Those who keep the law are wise children, but companions of gluttons shame their parents.
text.Rotherham: He that keepeth instruction, is a son with discernment, but, a companion of squanderers, bringeth shame to his father.
text.KJV: Whoso keepeth the law is a wise son: but he that is a companion of riotous men shameth his father.
text.NABRE: Whoever heeds instruction is a wise son, but whoever joins with wastrels disgraces his father.
text.DRB: He that keepeth the law is a wise son, but he that feedeth gluttons, shameth his father.

text.NRSV: One who augments wealth by exorbitant interest gathers it for another who is kind to the poor.
text.Rotherham: He that increaseth his substance by interest and profit, for one ready to favour the poor, doth gather it.
text.KJV: He that by usury and unjust gain increaseth his substance, he shall gather it for him that will pity the poor.
text.NABRE: Whoever amasses wealth by interest and overcharge gathers it for the one who is kind to the poor.
text.DRB: He that heapeth together riches by usuries and ocker, gathereth them for him that is liberal to the poor.

text.NRSV: When one will not listen to the law, even one’s prayers are an abomination.
text.Rotherham: He that turneth away his ear from hearing instruction, even his prayer, is an abomination.
text.KJV: He that turneth away his ear from hearing the law, even his prayer shall be abomination.
text.NABRE: Those who turn their ears from hearing instruction, even their prayer is an abomination.
text.DRB: He that turneth away his ears from hearing the law, his prayer shall be execrable.

text.NRSV: Those who mislead the upright into evil ways will fall into pits of their own making, but the blameless will have a goodly inheritance.
text.Rotherham: He that misguideth the upright into a hurtful way! into his own pit, he himself, shall fall, but, men of integrity, shall inherit good.
text.KJV: Whoso causeth the righteous to go astray in an evil way, he shall fall himself into his own pit: but the upright shall have good things in possession.
text.NABRE: Those who mislead the upright into an evil way will themselves fall into their own pit, but the blameless will attain prosperity.
text.DRB: He that deceiveth the just in a wicked way, shall fall into his destruction: and the simple shall possess his goods.

text.NRSV: The rich is wise in self-esteem, but an intelligent poor person sees through the poses.
text.Rotherham: Wise in his own eyes, is the man that is rich, but, a poor man of discernment, searcheth him out.
text.KJV: The rich man is wise in his own conceit; but the poor that hath understanding searcheth him out.
text.NABRE: The rich are wise in their own eyes, but the poor who are intelligent see through them.
text.DRB: The rich man seemeth to himself wise: but the poor man being prudent shall search him.

text.NRSV: When the righteous triumph, there is great glory, but when the wicked rise, people hide themselves.
text.Rotherham: In the exulting of the righteous, there is great glorying, but, when the lawless arise, a man must be sought for.
text.KJV: When righteous men do rejoice, there is great glory: but when the wicked rise, a man is hidden.
text.NABRE: When the just triumph, there is great glory; but when the wicked prevail, people hide.
text.DRB: In the exultation of the just there is much glory: when the impious reign ruins of men.

text.NRSV: No one who conceals transgressions will prosper, but one who confesses and forsakes them will obtain mercy.
text.Rotherham: He that covereth his transgressions, shall not prosper, but, he that confesseth and forsaketh, shall find compassion.
text.KJV: He that covereth his sins shall not prosper: but whoso confesseth and forsaketh them shall have mercy.
text.NABRE: Those who conceal their sins do not prosper, but those who confess and forsake them obtain mercy.
text.DRB: He that hideth his wicked deeds, shall not be directed: but he that shall confess, and shall forsake them, shall obtain mercy.

text.NRSV: Happy is the one who fears the Lord always, but the stubborn of heart will fall into calamity.
text.Rotherham: How happy the man who is ever circumspect, whereas, he that hardeneth his heart, shall fall into calamity.
text.KJV: Happy is the man that feareth alway: but he that hardeneth his heart shall fall into mischief.
text.NABRE: Happy those who always fear; but those who harden their hearts fall into evil.
text.DRB: Blessed is the man that is always fearful: but he that is of an obstinate mind, shall fall into evil.

text.NRSV: Like a roaring lion or a charging bear is a wicked ruler over a poor people.
text.Rotherham: A growling lion, and a ranging bear, is a lawless ruler, over a poor people.
text.KJV: As a roaring lion, and a ranging bear; so is a wicked ruler over the poor people.
text.NABRE: A roaring lion or a ravenous bear is a wicked ruler over a poor people.
text.DRB: Aroaring lion, and hungry bear, an impious Prince over the poor people.

text.NRSV: A ruler who lacks understanding is a cruel oppressor; but one who hates unjust gain will enjoy a long life.
text.Rotherham: A leader, may lack intelligence, yet abound in oppressions, The hater of greed, shall lengthen out days.
text.KJV: The prince that wanteth understanding is also a great oppressor: but he that hateth covetousness shall prolong his days.
text.NABRE: The less prudent the rulers, the more oppressive their deeds. Those who hate ill-gotten gain prolong their days.
text.DRB: A Prince lacking prudence, shall oppress many by calumny: but he that hateth avarice, his days shall be made long.

text.NRSV: If someone is burdened with the blood of another, let that person be a fugitive until death; let no one help.
text.Rotherham: A man oppressed with a person’s blood, unto a pit, shall flee, let them not hold him back.
text.KJV: A man that doeth violence to the blood of any person shall flee to the pit; let no man stay him.
text.NABRE: Though a person burdened with blood guilt is in flight even to the grave, let no one offer support.
text.DRB: Aman that doth calumniate the blood of a soul, if he shall fly into a lake, no man abideth.

text.NRSV: One who walks in integrity will be safe, but whoever follows crooked ways will fall into the Pit.
text.Rotherham: He that walketh with integrity, shall be saved, but, he that is crooked, turning two ways, shall fall in one.
text.KJV: Whoso walketh uprightly shall be saved: but he that is perverse in his ways shall fall at once.
text.NABRE: Whoever walks blamelessly is safe, but one whose ways are crooked falls into a pit.
text.DRB: He that walketh simply shall be saved: he that goeth perverse ways, shall fall once.

text.NRSV: Anyone who tills the land will have plenty of bread, but one who follows worthless pursuits will have plenty of poverty.
text.Rotherham: He that tilleth his ground, shall have plenty of bread, but, he that pursueth empty-heads, shall have plenty of poverty.
