text.KJV: He that tilleth his land shall have plenty of bread: but he that followeth after vain persons shall have poverty enough.
text.NABRE: Those who cultivate their land will have plenty of food, but those who engage in idle pursuits will have plenty of want.
text.DRB: He that tilleth his ground, shall be filled with breads: but he that pursueth idleness, shall be replenished with poverty.

text.NRSV: The faithful will abound with blessings, but one who is in a hurry to be rich will not go unpunished.
text.Rotherham: A man of fidelity, aboundeth in blessings, but, one hasting to be rich, shall not be held innocent.
text.KJV: A faithful man shall abound with blessings: but he that maketh haste to be rich shall not be innocent.
text.NABRE: The trustworthy will be richly blessed; but whoever hastens to be rich will not go unpunished.
text.DRB: Afaithful man shall be much praised: but he that hasteneth to be rich, shall not be innocent.

text.NRSV: To show partiality is not good— yet for a piece of bread a person may do wrong.
text.Rotherham: To take note of faces [in judgment], is not good, and, for a bit of bread, a man will transgress.
text.KJV: To have respect of persons is not good: for for a piece of bread that man will transgress.
text.NABRE: To show partiality is never good: for even a morsel of bread one may do wrong.
text.DRB: He that knoweth a person in judgement, doth not well: this man even for a morsel of bread forsaketh the truth.

text.NRSV: The miser is in a hurry to get rich and does not know that loss is sure to come.
text.Rotherham: A man, hasting to be rich, hath an evil eye, and knoweth not when want may overtake him.
text.KJV: He that hasteth to be rich hath an evil eye, and considereth not that poverty shall come upon him.
text.NABRE: Misers hurry toward wealth, not knowing that want is coming toward them.
text.DRB: Aman that hasteneth to be rich, and envieth others, is ignorant that poverty shall come upon him.

text.NRSV: Whoever rebukes another will afterward find more favor than one who flatters with the tongue.
text.Rotherham: He that reproveth a man, shall, afterwards, find more, favour, than he that useth a flattering tongue.
text.KJV: He that rebuketh a man afterwards shall find more favour than he that flattereth with the tongue.
text.NABRE: Whoever rebukes another wins more favor than one who flatters with the tongue.
text.DRB: He that rebuketh a man, shall afterward find favour with him, more than he that by flatteries of tongue deceiveth.

text.NRSV: Anyone who robs father or mother and says, “That is no transgression,” is a partner to one who destroys.
text.Rotherham: He that robbeth his father or his mother, and saith, It is no transgression, companion, is he to one who wasteth.
text.KJV: Whoso robbeth his father or his mother, and saith, It is no transgression; the same is the companion of a destroyer.
text.NABRE: Whoever defrauds father or mother and says, “It is no sin,” is a partner to a brigand.
text.DRB: He that pilfereth any thing from his father, and from his mother: & saith this is no sin, is the partaker of a mankiller.

text.NRSV: The greedy person stirs up strife, but whoever trusts in the Lord will be enriched.
text.Rotherham: The ambitious in soul, stirreth up strife, but, he that trusteth in Yahweh, shall be enriched.
text.KJV: He that is of a proud heart stirreth up strife: but he that putteth his trust in the Lord shall be made fat.
text.NABRE: The greedy person stirs up strife, but the one who trusts in the Lord will prosper.
text.DRB: He that exalteth and dilateth himself, raiseth brawls: but he that trusteth in our Lord, shall be healed.

text.NRSV: Those who trust in their own wits are fools, but those who walk in wisdom come through safely.
text.Rotherham: He that trusteth his own heart, the same, is a dullard, but, he that walketh wisely, the same, shall be delivered.
text.KJV: He that trusteth in his own heart is a fool: but whoso walketh wisely, he shall be delivered.
text.NABRE: Those who trust in themselves are fools, but those who walk in wisdom are safe.
text.DRB: He that hath confidence in his own heart, is a fool: but who so walketh wisely shall be saved.

text.NRSV: Whoever gives to the poor will lack nothing, but one who turns a blind eye will get many a curse.
text.Rotherham: One who giveth to the poor, shall have no want, but, he that hideth his eyes, shall receive many a curse.
text.KJV: He that giveth unto the poor shall not lack: but he that hideth his eyes shall have many a curse.
text.NABRE: Those who give to the poor have no lack, but those who avert their eyes, many curses.
text.DRB: He that giveth to the poor, shall not lack: he that despiseth him that asketh shall sustain penury.

text.NRSV: When the wicked rise, people hide themselves, but when they perish, the righteous increase.
text.Rotherham: When lawless men rise, a common man will hide himself, but, when they perish, righteous men multiply.
text.KJV: When the wicked rise, men hide themselves: but when they perish, the righteous increase.
text.NABRE: When the wicked prevail, people hide; but at their fall the just abound.
text.DRB: When the impious shall rise, men shall be hid: when they shall perish, the just shall be multiplied.

text.NRSV: One who is often reproved, yet remains stiff-necked, will suddenly be broken beyond repair.
text.Rotherham: He that being often reproved stiffeneth his neck, suddenly shall be hurt, and there be no healing.
text.KJV: He, that being often reproved hardeneth his neck, shall suddenly be destroyed, and that without remedy.
text.NABRE: Chapter 29 Those stiff-necked in the face of reproof in an instant will be shattered beyond cure.
text.DRB: The man that with stiff neck contemneth him that rebuketh, sudden destruction shall come upon him: and health shall not follow him.

text.NRSV: When the righteous are in authority, the people rejoice; but when the wicked rule, the people groan.
text.Rotherham: When the righteous become great, the people rejoice, but, when a lawless man beareth rule, a people sigh.
text.KJV: When the righteous are in authority, the people rejoice: but when the wicked beareth rule, the people mourn.
text.NABRE: When the just flourish, the people rejoice; but when the wicked rule, the people groan.
text.DRB: In the multiplication of just men, the common people shall rejoice: when the impious shall take princedom, the people shall mourn.

text.NRSV: A child who loves wisdom makes a father glad, but to keep company with prostitutes is to squander one’s substance.
text.Rotherham: A man who loveth wisdom, gladdeneth his father, but, a companion of harlots, destroyeth wealth.
text.KJV: Whoso loveth wisdom rejoiceth his father: but he that keepeth company with harlots spendeth his substance.
text.NABRE: Whoever loves wisdom gives joy to his father, but whoever consorts with harlots squanders his wealth.
text.DRB: Aman that loveth wisdom, maketh his father glad: but he that maintaineth harlots, shall destroy his substance.

text.NRSV: By justice a king gives stability to the land, but one who makes heavy exactions ruins it.
text.Rotherham: A king, by justice, shall establish a land,—but, a man open to bribes, bringeth it to ruin.
text.KJV: The king by judgment establisheth the land: but he that receiveth gifts overthroweth it.
text.NABRE: By justice a king builds up the land; but one who raises taxes tears it down.
text.DRB: Ajust king setteth up the land, a covetous man shall destroy it.

text.NRSV: Whoever flatters a neighbor is spreading a net for the neighbor’s feet.
text.Rotherham: A man who flattereth his neighbour, spreadeth, a net, over his steps.
text.KJV: A man that flattereth his neighbour spreadeth a net for his feet.
text.NABRE: Those who speak flattery to their neighbor cast a net at their feet.
text.DRB: Aman that with fair and feigned words speaketh to his friend, spreadeth a net to his steps.

text.NRSV: In the transgression of the evil there is a snare, but the righteous sing and rejoice.
text.Rotherham: In the transgression of a wicked man, is a snare, but, the righteous, doth shout in triumph and rejoice.
text.KJV: In the transgression of an evil man there is a snare: but the righteous doth sing and rejoice.
text.NABRE: The sin of the wicked is a trap, but the just run along joyfully.
text.DRB: Asnare shall entangle the wicked man sinning: and the just shall praise and rejoice.

text.NRSV: The righteous know the rights of the poor; the wicked have no such understanding.
text.Rotherham: The righteous doth acknowledge the plea of the poor, but, the lawless, regardeth not knowledge.
text.KJV: The righteous considereth the cause of the poor: but the wicked regardeth not to know it.
text.NABRE: The just care for the cause of the poor; the wicked do not understand such care.
text.DRB: The just knoweth the cause of the poor: the impious is ignorant of knowledge.

text.NRSV: Scoffers set a city aflame, but the wise turn away wrath.
text.Rotherham: Men given to mockery, inflame a city,—but, wise men, turn away anger.
text.KJV: Scornful men bring a city into a snare: but wise men turn away wrath.
text.NABRE: Scoffers enflame the city, but the wise calm the fury.
text.DRB: Pestilent men dissipate a city: but the wise turn away fury.

text.NRSV: If the wise go to court with a fool, there is only fretting and laughing, and no rest.
text.Rotherham: A wise man pleading with a foolish man, whether he rage or laugh, there is no settlement.
text.KJV: If a wise man contendeth with a foolish man, whether he rage or laugh, there is no rest.
text.NABRE: If a wise person disputes with a fool, there is railing and ridicule but no resolution.
text.DRB: Awise man, if he contend with a fool, whether he be angry, or whether he laugh, shall not find rest.

text.NRSV: The bloodthirsty hate the blameless, and they seek the life of the upright.
text.Rotherham: Blood-thirsty men, hate the blameless man, and, as for the upright, they seek his life.
text.KJV: The bloodthirsty hate the upright: but the just seek his soul.
text.NABRE: The bloodthirsty hate the blameless, but the upright seek his life.
text.DRB: Men of blood hate the simple: but just men seek his soul.

text.NRSV: A fool gives full vent to anger, but the wise quietly holds it back.
text.Rotherham: All his anger, doth a dullard let go, but, a wise man, by keeping it back, stilleth it.
text.KJV: A fool uttereth all his mind: but a wise man keepeth it in till afterwards.
text.NABRE: Fools give vent to all their anger; but the wise, biding their time, control it.
text.DRB: Afool uttereth all his spirit: a wiseman deferreth and reserveth till afterward.

text.NRSV: If a ruler listens to falsehood, all his officials will be wicked.
text.Rotherham: When a ruler giveth heed to the word of falsehood, all his attendants, become lawless.
text.KJV: If a ruler hearken to lies, all his servants are wicked.
text.NABRE: If rulers listen to lying words, their servants all become wicked.
text.DRB: A Prince that gladly heareth words of lying, hath all his servants impious.

text.NRSV: The poor and the oppressor have this in common: the Lord gives light to the eyes of both.
text.Rotherham: The poor man and the man of usury, meet together, he that enlighteneth the eyes of them both, is Yahweh.
text.KJV: The poor and the deceitful man meet together: the Lord lighteneth both their eyes.
text.NABRE: The poor and the oppressor meet: the Lord gives light to the eyes of both.
text.DRB: The poor man and the creditor have met one another: our Lord is illuminator of both.

text.NRSV: If a king judges the poor with equity, his throne will be established forever.
text.Rotherham: When a king judgeth faithfully the poor, his throne, to futurity, shall be established.
text.KJV: The king that faithfully judgeth the poor, his throne shall be established for ever.
text.NABRE: If a king is honestly for the rights of the poor, his throne stands firm forever.
text.DRB: The king that judgeth the poor in truth, his throne shall be replenished for ever.

text.NRSV: The rod and reproof give wisdom, but a mother is disgraced by a neglected child.
text.Rotherham: A rod with rebuke, giveth wisdom, but, a youth unrestrained, bringeth shame to his mother.
text.KJV: The rod and reproof give wisdom: but a child left to himself bringeth his mother to shame.
text.NABRE: The rod of correction gives wisdom, but uncontrolled youths disgrace their mothers.
text.DRB: Rod and rebuke giveth wisdom: but the child that is left to his own will, confoundeth his mother.

text.NRSV: When the wicked are in authority, transgression increases, but the righteous will look upon their downfall.
text.Rotherham: When the lawless become great, transgression increaseth, but, the righteous, shall behold, their ruin.
text.KJV: When the wicked are multiplied, transgression increaseth: but the righteous shall see their fall.
text.NABRE: When the wicked increase, crime increases; but the just will behold their downfall.
text.DRB: In the multiplication of the impious, wickedness shall be multiplied, and the just shall see the ruins of them.

text.NRSV: Discipline your children, and they will give you rest; they will give delight to your heart.
text.Rotherham: Correct thy son, that he may give thee rest, that he may give delight to thy soul.
text.KJV: Correct thy son, and he shall give thee rest; yea, he shall give delight unto thy soul.
text.NABRE: Discipline your children, and they will bring you comfort, and give delight to your soul.
text.DRB: Nurture thy son, and he shall refresh thee, and shall give delights to thy soul.

text.NRSV: Where there is no prophecy, the people cast off restraint, but happy are those who keep the law.
text.Rotherham: Where there is no vision, a people is let loose, but, he that keepeth instruction, how happy is he!
text.KJV: Where there is no vision, the people perish: but he that keepeth the law, happy is he.
text.NABRE: Without a vision the people lose restraint; but happy is the one who follows instruction.
text.DRB: When prophecy shall fail, the people shall be dissipated: but he that keepeth the Law, is blessed.

text.NRSV: By mere words servants are not disciplined, for though they understand, they will not give heed.
text.Rotherham: By words, a servant will not be corrected, though he perceiveth, yet is there no answer.
text.KJV: A servant will not be corrected by words: for though he understand he will not answer.
text.NABRE: Not by words alone can servants be trained; for they understand but do not respond.
text.DRB: Aservant cannot be taught by words: because he understandeth that which thou sayst, and contemneth to answer.

text.NRSV: Do you see someone who is hasty in speaking? There is more hope for a fool than for such a person.
text.Rotherham: Thou hast seen a man hasty in his words,—there is, more hope of a dullard, than of him.
text.KJV: Seest thou a man that is hasty in his words? there is more hope of a fool than of him.
text.NABRE: Do you see someone hasty in speech? There is more hope for a fool!
text.DRB: Hast thou seen a man swift to speak? Folly is rather to be hoped, than his amendment.

text.NRSV: A slave pampered from childhood will become a rebel in the end.
text.Rotherham: He that dealeth tenderly with his servant from childhood, in his after life, shall have him for a son.
text.KJV: He that delicately bringeth up his servant from a child shall have him become his son at the length.
text.NABRE: If servants are pampered from childhood they will turn out to be stubborn.
text.DRB: He that nourished his servant delicately from his childhood, afterward shall feel him stubborn.

text.NRSV: One given to anger stirs up strife, and the hot-tempered person causes many a transgression.
text.Rotherham: A man given to anger, stirreth up strife, and, he that exceedeth in wrath, aboundeth in transgression.
text.KJV: An angry man stirreth up strife, and a furious man aboundeth in transgression.
text.NABRE: The ill-tempered stir up strife, and the hotheaded cause many sins.
text.DRB: An angry man provoketh brawls: and he that is easy to indignation, shall be more prone to sin.

text.NRSV: A person’s pride will bring humiliation, but one who is lowly in spirit will obtain honor.
text.Rotherham: The loftiness of a man, layeth him low,—but, one of a lowly spirit, shall attain unto honour.
text.KJV: A man’s pride shall bring him low: but honour shall uphold the humble in spirit.
text.NABRE: Haughtiness brings humiliation, but the humble of spirit acquire honor.
text.DRB: Humiliation followeth the proud: and glory shall receive the humble of spirit.

text.NRSV: To be a partner of a thief is to hate one’s own life; one hears the victim’s curse, but discloses nothing.
text.Rotherham: He that shareth with a thief, hateth himself, an oath, he heareth, yet may not tell.
text.KJV: Whoso is partner with a thief hateth his own soul: he heareth cursing, and bewrayeth it not.
text.NABRE: Partners of a thief hate themselves; they hear the imprecation but do not testify.
text.DRB: He that is partaker with a thief, hateth his own soul: he heareth one adjuring, and telleth not.

text.NRSV: The fear of others lays a snare, but one who trusts in the Lord is secure.
text.Rotherham: The fear of man, setteth a snare, but, he that trusteth in Yahweh, shall be placed on high.
text.KJV: The fear of man bringeth a snare: but whoso putteth his trust in the Lord shall be safe.
text.NABRE: Fear of others becomes a snare, but the one who trusts in the Lord is safe.
text.DRB: He that feareth man, shall soon fall: he that trusteth in our Lord shall be lifted up.

text.NRSV: Many seek the favor of a ruler, but it is from the Lord that one gets justice.
text.Rotherham: Many, seek the face of a ruler, but, from Yahweh, is the sentence of each one.
text.KJV: Many seek the ruler’s favour; but every man’s judgment cometh from the Lord.
text.NABRE: Many curry favor with a ruler, but it is from the Lord that one receives justice.
text.DRB: Many seek after the face of the Prince: & the judgement of every one cometh forth from our Lord.

text.NRSV: The unjust are an abomination to the righteous, and those whose way is straight are an abomination to the wicked.
text.Rotherham: An abomination to the righteous, is the man of perversity, and, an abomination to the lawless, is a man of straightforward way.
text.KJV: An unjust man is an abomination to the just: and he that is upright in the way is abomination to the wicked.
text.NABRE: An abomination to the just, the evildoer; an abomination to the wicked, one whose way is straight.
text.DRB: The just abhor an impious man: & the impious abhor them that are in the right way. The son that keepeth the word, shall be out of perdition.

text.NRSV: The words of Agur son of Jakeh. An oracle. Thus says the man: I am weary, O God, I am weary, O God. How can I prevail?
text.Rotherham: The words of Agur, son of Jakeh, [even] the oracle,—The utterance of the man, for Ithiel, for Ithiel and Ucal.
text.KJV: The words of Agur the son of Jakeh, even the prophecy: the man spake unto Ithiel, even unto Ithiel and Ucal,
text.NABRE: VII. Sayings of Agur and Others Chapter 30 The words of Agur, son of Jakeh the Massaite: The pronouncement of mortal man: “I am weary, O God; I am weary, O God, and I am exhausted.
text.DRB: The words of the Gatherer the son of Vomiter. The vision, that the man spake, with whom God is, and who being strengthened by God abiding with him, said:

text.NRSV: Surely I am too stupid to be human; I do not have human understanding.
text.Rotherham: Surely, more brutish, am, I, than any man, nor doth, the understanding of a son of earth, pertain to me;
text.KJV: Surely I am more brutish than any man, and have not the understanding of a man.
text.NABRE: I am more brute than human being, without even human intelligence;
text.DRB: I am most foolish of men, & the wisdom of men is not with me.

text.NRSV: I have not learned wisdom, nor have I knowledge of the holy ones.
text.Rotherham: Neither have I learned wisdom, nor, the knowledge of the Holy Ones, can I acquire.
text.KJV: I neither learned wisdom, nor have the knowledge of the holy.
text.NABRE: Neither have I learned wisdom, nor have I the knowledge of the Holy One.
text.DRB: I have not learned wisdom, and have not known the science of saints.

text.NRSV: Who has ascended to heaven and come down? Who has gathered the wind in the hollow of the hand? Who has wrapped up the waters in a garment? Who has established all the ends of the earth? What is the name, and what is the name of the child? Surely you know!
text.Rotherham: Who hath ascended the heavens and then descended? Who hath gathered the wind into his two hands? Who hath wrapped up the waters in a mantle? Who hath set up all the ends of the earth? What is his name and what the name of his son, when thou knowest?
text.KJV: Who hath ascended up into heaven, or descended? who hath gathered the wind in his fists? who hath bound the waters in a garment? who hath established all the ends of the earth? what is his name, and what is his son’s name, if thou canst tell?
text.NABRE: Who has gone up to heaven and come down again— who has cupped the wind in the hollow of the hand? Who has bound up the waters in a cloak— who has established all the ends of the earth? What is that person’s name, or the name of his son?”
text.DRB: Who hath ascended into heaven and descended? Who hath contained the spirit in his hands? Who hath bound the waters together as in a garment? Who hath raised up all the borders of the earth? What is his name, and what is the name of his son, if thou know?

text.NRSV: Every word of God proves true; he is a shield to those who take refuge in him.
text.Rotherham: Every saying of God is refined, A shield, is, he, to them who seek refuge in him.
text.KJV: Every word of God is pure: he is a shield unto them that put their trust in him.
text.NABRE: Every word of God is tested; he is a shield to those who take refuge in him.
text.DRB: Every word of God tried by fire, is a buckler to them that hope in him:

text.NRSV: Do not add to his words, or he will rebuke you, and you will be found a liar.
text.Rotherham: Do not add unto his words, lest he convict thee, and thou be found false.
text.KJV: Add thou not unto his words, lest he reprove thee, and thou be found a liar.
text.NABRE: Add nothing to his words, lest he reprimand you, and you be proved a liar.
text.DRB: Add not any thing to his words, and so thou be reproved and found a liar.

text.NRSV: Two things I ask of you; do not deny them to me before I die:
text.Rotherham: Two things, have I asked of thee, withhold them not from me, ere yet I die:
text.KJV: Two things have I required of thee; deny me them not before I die:
text.NABRE: Two things I ask of you, do not deny them to me before I die:
text.DRB: Two things I have asked thee, deny them not to me before I die.

text.NRSV: Remove far from me falsehood and lying; give me neither poverty nor riches; feed me with the food that I need,
text.Rotherham: Vanity and falsehood, remove far from me, Neither poverty nor riches, give me, Feed me with the food appointed me:
text.KJV: Remove far from me vanity and lies: give me neither poverty nor riches; feed me with food convenient for me:
text.NABRE: Put falsehood and lying far from me, give me neither poverty nor riches; provide me only with the food I need;
text.DRB: Vanity, and lying words make far from me. Beggary, and riches give me not: give only things necessary for my sustenance:

text.NRSV: or I shall be full, and deny you, and say, “Who is the Lord?” or I shall be poor, and steal, and profane the name of my God.
text.Rotherham: Lest I be full, and deny, and say—Who is Yahweh? or lest I be impoverished and steal, and do violence to the Name of my God.
text.KJV: Lest I be full, and deny thee, and say, Who is the Lord? or lest I be poor, and steal, and take the name of my God in vain.
text.NABRE: Lest, being full, I deny you, saying, “Who is the Lord?” Or, being in want, I steal, and profane the name of my God.
text.DRB: lest perhaps being filled I be allured to deny, and may say: Who is the Lord? Or being compelled by poverty I may steal, and forswear the name of my God.

text.NRSV: Do not slander a servant to a master, or the servant will curse you, and you will be held guilty.
text.Rotherham: Do not slander a servant to his master, lest he revile thee, and thou be found guilty.
text.KJV: Accuse not a servant unto his master, lest he curse thee, and thou be found guilty.
text.NABRE: Do not criticize servants to their master, lest they curse you, and you have to pay the penalty.
text.DRB: Accuse not a servant to his master, lest perhaps he curse thee, and thou fall.

text.NRSV: There are those who curse their fathers and do not bless their mothers.
text.Rotherham: A generation! Its father, it revileth, and, its mother, it doth not bless.
text.KJV: There is a generation that curseth their father, and doth not bless their mother.
text.NABRE: There are some who curse their fathers, and do not bless their mothers.
text.DRB: There is a generation that curseth their father, and that blesseth not their mother.

text.NRSV: There are those who are pure in their own eyes yet are not cleansed of their filthiness.
text.Rotherham: A generation! Pure in its own eyes, yet, from its filth, hath it not been bathed,
text.KJV: There is a generation that are pure in their own eyes, and yet is not washed from their filthiness.
text.NABRE: There are some pure in their own eyes, yet not cleansed of their filth.
text.DRB: Ageneration, that seemeth to it-self clean, & yet is not washed from their filthiness.

text.NRSV: There are those—how lofty are their eyes, how high their eyelids lift!
text.Rotherham: A generation! How lofty are its eyes, and its eyelashes uplifted.
text.KJV: There is a generation, O how lofty are their eyes! and their eyelids are lifted up.
text.NABRE: There are some—how haughty their eyes! how overbearing their glance!
text.DRB: Ageneration, whose eyes are lofty, and the eye-lids thereof set up on high.

text.NRSV: There are those whose teeth are swords, whose teeth are knives, to devour the poor from off the earth, the needy from among mortals.
text.Rotherham: A generation! Swords, are its teeth, and, knives, its incisors,—to devour the humbled out of the earth, and the needy, from among men.
text.KJV: There is a generation, whose teeth are as swords, and their jaw teeth as knives, to devour the poor from off the earth, and the needy from among men.
text.NABRE: There are some—their teeth are swords, their teeth are knives, Devouring the needy from the earth, and the poor from the human race.
text.DRB: Ageneration, that for teeth hath swords, and chaweth with their grinding teeth, that they may eat the needy out of the earth, and the poor from among men.

text.NRSV: The leech has two daughters; “Give, give,” they cry. Three things are never satisfied; four never say, “Enough”:
text.Rotherham: The vampire, hath two daughters, Give! Give! Three, there are will not be satisfied, four, have not said, Enough!
text.KJV: The horseleach hath two daughters, crying, Give, give. There are three things that are never satisfied, yea, four things say not, It is enough:
text.NABRE: The leech has two daughters: “Give,” and “Give.” Three things never get their fill, four never say, “Enough!”
text.DRB: The horseleech hath two daughters that say: Bring, bring. Three things are unsatiable, the fourth never saith it sufficeth.

text.NRSV: Sheol, the barren womb, the earth ever thirsty for water, and the fire that never says, “Enough.”
text.Rotherham: Hades, and barrenness,—A land not satisfied with water, and fire, that saith not, Enough!
text.KJV: The grave; and the barren womb; the earth that is not filled with water; and the fire that saith not, It is enough.
text.NABRE: Sheol, a barren womb, land that never gets its fill of water, and fire, which never says, “Enough!”
text.DRB: Hell, and the mouth of the matrice, & the earth which is not satisfied with water: but the fire never saith it sufficeth.

text.NRSV: The eye that mocks a father and scorns to obey a mother will be pecked out by the ravens of the valley and eaten by the vultures.
text.Rotherham: The eye that mocketh a father, and despiseth to obey a mother, the ravens of the valley shall pick it out, and the young of the eagle shall eat it.
text.KJV: The eye that mocketh at his father, and despiseth to obey his mother, the ravens of the valley shall pick it out, and the young eagles shall eat it.
text.NABRE: The eye that mocks a father, or scorns the homage due a mother, Will be plucked out by brook ravens; devoured by a brood of vultures.
text.DRB: The eye that scorneth his father, & that despiseth the travail of his mother in bearing him, let the ravens of the torrents pick it out, and the young of the eagle eat it.

text.NRSV: Three things are too wonderful for me; four I do not understand:
text.Rotherham: Three, things there are, too difficult for me, yea, four, which I do not understand:
text.KJV: There be three things which are too wonderful for me, yea, four which I know not:
text.NABRE: Three things are too wonderful for me, yes, four I cannot understand:
text.DRB: Three things are hard to me, and of the fourth I am utterly ignorant.

text.NRSV: the way of an eagle in the sky, the way of a serpent on a rock, the way of a ship on the high seas, and the way of a man with a girl.
text.Rotherham: The way of an eagle in the heavens, the way of a serpent on the rock,—the way of a ship in the heart of the sea, and the way of a man with a maid.
text.KJV: The way of an eagle in the air; the way of a serpent upon a rock; the way of a ship in the midst of the sea; and the way of a man with a maid.
text.NABRE: The way of an eagle in the sky, the way of a serpent upon a rock, The way of a ship on the high seas, and the way of a man with a woman.
text.DRB: The way of an eagle in the air, the way of a serpent upon a rock, the way of a ship in the midst of the sea, and the way of a man in youth.

text.NRSV: This is the way of an adulteress: she eats, and wipes her mouth, and says, “I have done no wrong.”
text.Rotherham: So, is the way of a woman committing adultery,—she eateth, and wipeth her mouth, and saith, I have done no iniquity!
text.KJV: Such is the way of an adulterous woman; she eateth, and wipeth her mouth, and saith, I have done no wickedness.
text.NABRE: This is the way of an adulterous woman: she eats, wipes her mouth, and says, “I have done no wrong.”
text.DRB: Such is also the way of an adulterous woman, which eateth, and wiping her mouth saith: I have done no evil.

text.NRSV: Under three things the earth trembles; under four it cannot bear up:
text.Rotherham: Under three things, a land is stirred, yea, under four, she cannot bear up:
text.KJV: For three things the earth is disquieted, and for four which it cannot bear:
text.NABRE: Under three things the earth trembles, yes, under four it cannot bear up:
text.DRB: By three things the earth is moved, and the fourth it cannot sustain.

text.NRSV: a slave when he becomes king, and a fool when glutted with food;
text.Rotherham: Under a servant, when he reigneth, and a base man, when he is surfeited with food;
text.KJV: For a servant when he reigneth; and a fool when he is filled with meat;
text.NABRE: Under a slave who becomes king, and a fool who is glutted with food;
text.DRB: By a servant when he shall reign: by a fool when he shall be filled with meat:

text.NRSV: an unloved woman when she gets a husband, and a maid when she succeeds her mistress.
text.Rotherham: Under a hateful woman, when she is married, and a handmaid when she driveth out her mistress.
text.KJV: For an odious woman when she is married; and an handmaid that is heir to her mistress.
text.NABRE: Under an unloved woman who is wed, and a maidservant who displaces her mistress.
text.DRB: by an odious woman when she shall be taken in matrimony: & by a bond-woman when she shall be heir to her mistress.

text.NRSV: Four things on earth are small, yet they are exceedingly wise:
text.Rotherham: Four, things there are, the small of the earth,—yet, they, are wiser than the wise:
text.KJV: There be four things which are little upon the earth, but they are exceeding wise:
text.NABRE: Four things are among the smallest on the earth, and yet are exceedingly wise:
text.DRB: There are four the least things of the earth, and they are wiser than the wise.

text.NRSV: the ants are a people without strength, yet they provide their food in the summer;
text.Rotherham: The ants, a people, not strong,—yet prepare they, in summer, their food;
text.KJV: The ants are a people not strong, yet they prepare their meat in the summer;
text.NABRE: Ants—a species not strong, yet they store up their food in the summer;
text.DRB: The ants, a weak people, which prepareth in the harvest meat for themselves:

text.NRSV: the badgers are a people without power, yet they make their homes in the rocks;
text.Rotherham: The conies, a people of, no power, yet set they, among the crags, their house;
text.KJV: The conies are but a feeble folk, yet make they their houses in the rocks;
text.NABRE: Badgers—a species not mighty, yet they make their home in the crags;
text.DRB: The leveret, a people not strong, which placeth his bed in the rock:

text.NRSV: the locusts have no king, yet all of them march in rank;
text.Rotherham: King, is there none, to, the locusts,—yet go forth in swarms, do they all;
text.KJV: The locusts have no king, yet go they forth all of them by bands;
text.NABRE: Locusts—they have no king, yet they march forth in formation;
text.DRB: The locust hath no king, and they go out all by their troops:

text.NRSV: the lizard can be grasped in the hand, yet it is found in kings’ palaces.
text.Rotherham: The lizard, with hands, reneweth its hold,—yet, the same, is in the palaces of the king.
text.KJV: The spider taketh hold with her hands, and is in kings’ palaces.
text.NABRE: Lizards—you can catch them with your hands, yet they find their way into kings’ palaces.
text.DRB: The stellion stayeth on his hands, & tarrieth in king's houses.

text.NRSV: Three things are stately in their stride; four are stately in their gait:
text.Rotherham: Three, things there are which step along well, ye, four, which excel in going:
text.KJV: There be three things which go well, yea, four are comely in going:
text.NABRE: Three things are stately in their stride, yes, four are stately in their carriage:
text.DRB: There are three things which go well, and the fourth that goeth happily.

text.NRSV: the lion, which is mightiest among wild animals and does not turn back before any;
text.Rotherham: The lion, hero among beasts, which turneth aside from the face of no one;
text.KJV: A lion which is strongest among beasts, and turneth not away for any;
text.NABRE: The lion, mightiest of beasts, retreats before nothing;
text.DRB: The lion, the strongest of beasts shall fear at the meeting of none:

text.NRSV: the strutting cock, the he-goat, and a king striding before his people.
text.Rotherham: The greyhound, or the he-goat,—and a king, having a band of soldiers with him.
text.KJV: A greyhound; an he goat also; and a king, against whom there is no rising up.
text.NABRE: The strutting cock, and the he-goat, and the king at the head of his people.
text.DRB: the cock girded about the loins, and the ram: also the king, against whom none can resist.

text.NRSV: If you have been foolish, exalting yourself, or if you have been devising evil, put your hand on your mouth.
text.Rotherham: If thou hast acted basely by lifting thyself up,—if thou hast plotted evil, [put thy] hand to [thy] mouth!
text.KJV: If thou hast done foolishly in lifting up thyself, or if thou hast thought evil, lay thine hand upon thy mouth.
text.NABRE: If you have foolishly been proud or presumptuous—put your hand on your mouth;
text.DRB: There is that hath appeared a fool after that he was lifted up on high: for if he had understood, he would have laid his hand upon his mouth.

text.NRSV: For as churning milk produces butter, and twisting the nose produces blood, so forcing anger produces strife.
text.Rotherham: Surely, the pressing of milk, bringeth forth curd, and, the pressing of the nose, bringeth forth blood, and, the pressing of wrath, bringeth forth strife.
text.KJV: Surely the churning of milk bringeth forth butter, and the wringing of the nose bringeth forth blood: so the forcing of wrath bringeth forth strife.
text.NABRE: For as the churning of milk produces curds, and the pressing of the nose produces blood, the churning of anger produces strife.
text.DRB: And he that strongly presseth the paps to wring out milk, straineth out butter, and he that violently cleanseth his nose, wringeth out blood: & he that provoketh angers, bringeth forth discords.

text.NRSV: The words of King Lemuel. An oracle that his mother taught him:
text.Rotherham: The words of Lemuel the king,—the strain which was taught him by his mother:—
text.KJV: The words of king Lemuel, the prophecy that his mother taught him.
text.NABRE: VIII. Sayings of King Lemuel Chapter 31 The words of Lemuel, king of Massa, the instruction his mother taught him:
text.DRB: The words of Lamuel the king. The vision wherewith his mother instructed him.

text.NRSV: No, my son! No, son of my womb! No, son of my vows!
text.Rotherham: What, my son? and what, the son of my womb? aye what, the son of my vows?
text.KJV: What, my son? and what, the son of my womb? and what, the son of my vows?
text.NABRE: What are you doing, my son! what are you doing, son of my womb; what are you doing, son of my vows!
text.DRB: What o my beloved, what o the beloved of my womb, what o beloved of my vows?

text.NRSV: Do not give your strength to women, your ways to those who destroy kings.
text.Rotherham: Do not give, to women, thy strength, nor thy ways, to them who ruin kings.
text.KJV: Give not thy strength unto women, nor thy ways to that which destroyeth kings.
text.NABRE: Do not give your vigor to women, or your strength to those who ruin kings.
text.DRB: Give not thy substance to women, & thy riches to destroy kings.

text.NRSV: It is not for kings, O Lemuel, it is not for kings to drink wine, or for rulers to desire strong drink;
text.Rotherham: It is not for kings, O Lemuel, it is not for kings to drink wine, nor, for dignitaries, to desire strong drink,
text.KJV: It is not for kings, O Lemuel, it is not for kings to drink wine; nor for princes strong drink:
text.NABRE: It is not for kings, Lemuel, not for kings to drink wine; strong drink is not for princes,
text.DRB: Give not to kings, o Lamuel, give not wine to kings: because there is no secret where drunkenness reigneth,

text.NRSV: or else they will drink and forget what has been decreed, and will pervert the rights of all the afflicted.
text.Rotherham: Lest he drink, and forget that which is decreed, and alter the plea of any who are sorely oppressed.
text.KJV: Lest they drink, and forget the law, and pervert the judgment of any of the afflicted.
text.NABRE: Lest in drinking they forget what has been decreed, and violate the rights of any who are in need.
text.DRB: & lest perhaps they drink, & forget judgments, & change the cause of the children of the poor.

text.NRSV: Give strong drink to one who is perishing, and wine to those in bitter distress;
text.Rotherham: Give strong drink, to him that is perishing, and wine, to such as are embittered in soul:
text.KJV: Give strong drink unto him that is ready to perish, and wine unto those that be of heavy hearts.
text.NABRE: Give strong drink to anyone who is perishing, and wine to the embittered;
text.DRB: Give strong drink to them that be sad, and wine unto them that are of a pensive mind:

text.NRSV: let them drink and forget their poverty, and remember their misery no more.
text.Rotherham: Let him drink, and forget his poverty, and, his wearying toil, let him remember no more.
text.KJV: Let him drink, and forget his poverty, and remember his misery no more.
text.NABRE: When they drink, they will forget their misery, and think no more of their troubles.
text.DRB: let them drink, and forget their poverty, and not remember their sorrow any more.

text.NRSV: Speak out for those who cannot speak, for the rights of all the destitute.
text.Rotherham: Open thy mouth for the dumb, for the cause of all the children of the departed.
text.KJV: Open thy mouth for the dumb in the cause of all such as are appointed to destruction.
text.NABRE: Open your mouth in behalf of the mute, and for the rights of the destitute;
text.DRB: Open thy mouth to the dumb, & to the causes of all the children that pass:

text.NRSV: Speak out, judge righteously, defend the rights of the poor and needy.
text.Rotherham: Open thy mouth—judge righteously,—and administer justice for the poor and the needy.
text.KJV: Open thy mouth, judge righteously, and plead the cause of the poor and needy.
text.NABRE: Open your mouth, judge justly, defend the needy and the poor!
text.DRB: open thy mouth, decree that which is just, & judge the needy & poor.

text.NRSV: A capable wife who can find? She is far more precious than jewels.
text.Rotherham: A virtuous woman, who can find? for, far beyond corals, is her worth.
text.KJV: Who can find a virtuous woman? for her price is far above rubies.
text.NABRE: IX. Poem on the Woman of Worth Who can find a woman of worth? Far beyond jewels is her value.
text.DRB: Avaliant woman who shall find? Far, and from the utmost borders is the price of her.

text.NRSV: The heart of her husband trusts in her, and he will have no lack of gain.
text.Rotherham: The heart of her husband, trusteth her, and, gain, he shall not lack:
text.KJV: The heart of her husband doth safely trust in her, so that he shall have no need of spoil.
text.NABRE: Her husband trusts her judgment; he does not lack income.
text.DRB: The heart of her husband trusteth in her, and he shall not need spoils.

text.NRSV: She does him good, and not harm, all the days of her life.
text.Rotherham: She doeth him good and not evil, all the days of her life:
text.KJV: She will do him good and not evil all the days of her life.
text.NABRE: She brings him profit, not loss, all the days of her life.
text.DRB: She shall render good, and not evil, all the days of her life.

text.NRSV: She seeks wool and flax, and works with willing hands.
text.Rotherham: She seeketh wool and flax, and worketh willingly with her hands:
text.KJV: She seeketh wool, and flax, and worketh willingly with her hands.
text.NABRE: She seeks out wool and flax and weaves with skillful hands.
text.DRB: She hath sought wool and flax, and hath wrought by the counsel of her hands.

text.NRSV: She is like the ships of the merchant, she brings her food from far away.
text.Rotherham: She is like the ships of the merchant, from afar, she bringeth in her food;
text.KJV: She is like the merchants’ ships; she bringeth her food from afar.
text.NABRE: Like a merchant fleet, she secures her provisions from afar.
text.DRB: She is become as a merchant's ship, bringing her bread from far.

text.NRSV: She rises while it is still night and provides food for her household and tasks for her servant-girls.
text.Rotherham: And she riseth, while yet it is night, and giveth food to her household, and a task to her maidens:
text.KJV: She riseth also while it is yet night, and giveth meat to her household, and a portion to her maidens.
text.NABRE: She rises while it is still night, and distributes food to her household, a portion to her maidservants.
text.DRB: And she hath risen in the night, and given prey to her household, and meats to her handmaids.

text.NRSV: She considers a field and buys it; with the fruit of her hands she plants a vineyard.
text.Rotherham: She considereth a field, and procureth it, Out of the fruit of her hands, she planteth a vineyard:
text.KJV: She considereth a field, and buyeth it: with the fruit of her hands she planteth a vineyard.
text.NABRE: She picks out a field and acquires it; from her earnings she plants a vineyard.
text.DRB: She hath viewed a field, and bought it, of the fruit of her hands she hath planted a vineyard.

text.NRSV: She girds herself with strength, and makes her arms strong.
text.Rotherham: She girdeth, with strength her loins, and putteth vigour into her arms:
text.KJV: She girdeth her loins with strength, and strengtheneth her arms.
text.NABRE: She girds herself with strength; she exerts her arms with vigor.
text.DRB: She hath girded her loins with strength, and hath strengthened her arm.

text.NRSV: She perceives that her merchandise is profitable. Her lamp does not go out at night.
text.Rotherham: She tasteth, whether, good, be her merchandise, and her lamp, goeth not out by night:
text.KJV: She perceiveth that her merchandise is good: her candle goeth not out by night.
text.NABRE: She enjoys the profit from her dealings; her lamp is never extinguished at night.
text.DRB: She hath tasted, and seen that her traffic is good: her lamp shall not be extinguished in the night.

text.NRSV: She puts her hands to the distaff, and her hands hold the spindle.
text.Rotherham: Her hands, she putteth forth to the distaff, and, her palms, lay hold of the spindle:
text.KJV: She layeth her hands to the spindle, and her hands hold the distaff.
text.NABRE: She puts her hands to the distaff, and her fingers ply the spindle.
text.DRB: She hath put her hand to strong things, and her fingers have taken hold of the spindle.

text.NRSV: She opens her hand to the poor, and reaches out her hands to the needy.
text.Rotherham: Her palm, she spreadeth out to the oppressed, and, her hands, she extendeth to the needy:
text.KJV: She stretcheth out her hand to the poor; yea, she reacheth forth her hands to the needy.
text.NABRE: She reaches out her hands to the poor, and extends her arms to the needy.
text.DRB: She hath opened her hand to the needy, and stretched out her palms to the poor.

text.NRSV: She is not afraid for her household when it snows, for all her household are clothed in crimson.
text.Rotherham: She feareth not, for her household, because of the snow, for, all her household, are clothed with crimson:
text.KJV: She is not afraid of the snow for her household: for all her household are clothed with scarlet.
text.NABRE: She is not concerned for her household when it snows— all her charges are doubly clothed.
text.DRB: She shall not fear for her house in the colds of snow: for all her household are clothed with double.

text.NRSV: She makes herself coverings; her clothing is fine linen and purple.
text.Rotherham: Coverlets, she maketh for herself, Of white linen and of purple, is her clothing:
text.KJV: She maketh herself coverings of tapestry; her clothing is silk and purple.
text.NABRE: She makes her own coverlets; fine linen and purple are her clothing.
text.DRB: Tapestry clothing she hath made to herself: silk and purple is her garment.

text.NRSV: Her husband is known in the city gates, taking his seat among the elders of the land.
text.Rotherham: Known in the gates, is her husband, when he sitteth, with the elders of the land:
text.KJV: Her husband is known in the gates, when he sitteth among the elders of the land.
text.NABRE: Her husband is prominent at the city gates as he sits with the elders of the land.
text.DRB: Her husband is noble in the gates, when he shall sit with the senators of the land.

text.NRSV: She makes linen garments and sells them; she supplies the merchant with sashes.
text.Rotherham: Fine linen wraps, she maketh and selleth, and, girdles, doth she deliver to the trader:
text.KJV: She maketh fine linen, and selleth it; and delivereth girdles unto the merchant.
text.NABRE: She makes garments and sells them, and stocks the merchants with belts.
text.DRB: She made sindon, and sold it, and delivered a girdle to the Chananeite.

text.NRSV: Strength and dignity are her clothing, and she laughs at the time to come.
text.Rotherham: Strength and dignity, are her clothing, and she laugheth at the time to come:
text.KJV: Strength and honour are her clothing; and she shall rejoice in time to come.
text.NABRE: She is clothed with strength and dignity, and laughs at the days to come.
text.DRB: Strength and beauty is her garment, and she shall laugh in the later day.

text.NRSV: She opens her mouth with wisdom, and the teaching of kindness is on her tongue.
text.Rotherham: Her mouth, she openeth with wisdom, and, the instruction of kindness, is on her tongue:
text.KJV: She openeth her mouth with wisdom; and in her tongue is the law of kindness.
text.NABRE: She opens her mouth in wisdom; kindly instruction is on her tongue.
text.DRB: She hath opened her mouth to wisdom, and the law of clemency is in her tongue.

text.NRSV: She looks well to the ways of her household, and does not eat the bread of idleness.
text.Rotherham: She looketh well to the goings of her household, and, the bread of idleness, will she not eat.
text.KJV: She looketh well to the ways of her household, and eateth not the bread of idleness.
text.NABRE: She watches over the affairs of her household, and does not eat the bread of idleness.
text.DRB: She hath considered the paths of her house, and hath not eaten her bread idle.

text.NRSV: Her children rise up and call her happy; her husband also, and he praises her:
text.Rotherham: Her children rise up, and call her happy! her husband, and he praiseth her:—
text.KJV: Her children arise up, and call her blessed; her husband also, and he praiseth her.
text.NABRE: Her children rise up and call her blessed; her husband, too, praises her:
text.DRB: Her children arose, and commended her to be most blessed: her husband, and he praised her.

text.NRSV: “Many women have done excellently, but you surpass them all.”
text.Rotherham: Many daughters, have done virtuously, but, thou, excellest them all!
text.KJV: Many daughters have done virtuously, but thou excellest them all.
text.NABRE: “Many are the women of proven worth, but you have excelled them all.”
text.DRB: Many daughters have gathered together riches: thou hast passed them all.

text.NRSV: Charm is deceitful, and beauty is vain, but a woman who fears the Lord is to be praised.
text.Rotherham: Deceitful is loveliness and vain is beauty, The woman that revereth Yahweh, she, shall be praised:
text.KJV: Favour is deceitful, and beauty is vain: but a woman that feareth the Lord, she shall be praised.
text.NABRE: Charm is deceptive and beauty fleeting; the woman who fears the Lord is to be praised.
text.DRB: Grace is deceitful, and beauty is vain: the woman that feareth our Lord shall be praised.

text.NRSV: Give her a share in the fruit of her hands, and let her works praise her in the city gates.
text.Rotherham: Give her of the fruit of her own hands, and let her own works, praise her in the gates.
text.KJV: Give her of the fruit of her hands; and let her own works praise her in the gates.
text.NABRE: Acclaim her for the work of her hands, and let her deeds praise her at the city gates.
text.DRB: Give ye to her of the fruit of her hands, and let her works praise her in the gates.
